desc:
This large hall has a huge round mahogany table in the centre with many sturdy,
polished oak chairs placed around it. One of the chairs is decorated with
intricately carved designs resembling the figures of the Valar. In one corner of
the hall, there is a small table with water pitchers placed on it. The hall is
lit with four large torches burning in each corner. The room is apparently
designed to keep the outside noises from bothering people inside. Your senses
tell you to try and keep yourself from disturbing the silence.
name: Council Room

desc:
The wide, worn trail continues onward with the occasional tumbleweed blowing
across the path. Age and use have stamped out any potential foliage from growing
in the hard-packed soil. What secrets and wonder this old track must hold under
foot. The path continues to the north and west while the Tower Hills rise up to
the south and east attempting to swallow this ancient route.
name: Deeply-rutted Wagon Trail

desc:
The path turns and twists as it snakes around hills too steep for the road to
traverse. The old road consists of well-packed dirt with wagon ruts and scuffed
footprints spread across it. Small shrubs, interspersed with moss-covered rocks,
frame the path making the dark surface of the path stand out from the pastel
shades of the surrounding wilderness.
name: Deeply-rutted Wagon Trail

desc:
The ancient road that has withstood the test of time slowly bends to avoid a
steep drop. The road is wide; wide enough that two carts could easily pass each
other. On the surface of the road, ruts have formed from the years of wagon use,
most follow the same route but one set turns sharply south. A runaway wagon
appears to have left the road careering, out of control, towards the sheer drop
leaving a line of broken shrubs and skid marks in its wake. The tracks lead
directly over the cliff edge sealing the wagon's fate.
name: Deeply-rutted Wagon Trail

desc:
A wide path, created by countless wagons travelling the same route, follows the
valleys through these hills. Only a few weeds grow in the spaces between the
ruts perhaps indicating that this path sees regular travel. High, grass-clad
hills rise on both sides of the path. The trail continues to the east and west
while a narrow canyon blocks the way to the south.
name: A Deeply-rutted Wagon Trail

desc:
A wide trail cuts across the Tower Hills here. Wagons use this path with some
regularity, judging from the twin ruts worn into the hard ground. Grass and
weeds grow between the ruts and on the sides of the trail. On either side, the
hills rise and fall with no sign of significant change of the terrain for miles
in any direction.
name: A Deeply-rutted Wagon Trail

desc:
An old, worn path turns west and north here, making a sharp bend due to a tall,
green hill to the southeast. Deep, parallel grooves in the earth mark the
passage of numerous wagons that have come this way. Still, much of the path is
choked with grass and weeds and areas where wagons could overtake in the past
have been reduced to a single track.
name: A Deeply-rutted Wagon Trail

desc:
Following the bottom of the valleys, this partially weed-strewn path twists and
turns in deference to the surrounding landscape heading east and south. The
lands to the north and west look wild and untamed, completely unmarked by
civilisation while to the south and east the trail stretches into the distance.
name: A Deeply-rutted Wagon Trail

desc:
A worn trail can be found here, making its way between the hills and running
along valleys and the gentler slopes. The trail appears to be rather old and the
occasional plant has started to grow on it. It continues to the north and west.
To the south and east, a rocky scrabble that leads upward can be seen without
any way of navigating it.
name: Deeply-rutted Wagon Trail

desc:
Sweeping through the rolling grassland, the trail gently meanders along the
contours of the low hills, following the flat bottom of valleys. The trail is
marked by deep ruts, especially in the narrower sections where it bends around
the slopes of hills.
name: Deeply-rutted Wagon Trail

desc:
The trail runs along the base of a steep cliff before turning west across a
grassy hillside. It snakes its way across the landscape, leaving a thin brown
scar in the middle of the verdant, green grass. Rocks and small shrubs line the
trail on both sides.
name: Deeply-rutted Wagon Trail

desc:
Long and straight, the trail stretches out of sight to the east and west.
Rolling hills flank the trail forming a shallow valley that offers some
protection from the prevailing winds. Patches of flowers are the only flora,
other than the omnipresent grass, that has managed to grow in the infertile soil
alongside the track.
name: Deeply-rutted Wagon Trail

desc:
Far to the north-west, there is a large dip among the hills. The hills in the
immediate vicinity are small with gentle slopes. A trail winds its way from the
south to the east. The passage of wagons and travellers has worn away any
vegetation on the trail, but plenty of grass and wildflowers grow on either side
of this trail. There are a lot of animal paths and tracks around, making it
appear that plenty of deer and other game feed on the abundent grasses around
this trail.
name: Bending Wagon Trail

desc:
A wagon trail winds through the hills in a northern and western direction. The
hills are taller and rougher to the east and to the south. The hills are mostly
covered in grass. Various flowers, shrubs, and other plants also thrive wherever
the bedrock is covered by soil.
name: Winding Wagon Trail

desc:
The hills slope upward to the north and west just off of the trail that passes
through here. The grass-covered valley that extends to the east and south forms
a natural break from the hills that the trail follows. The grass covering the
hills flow down from them and covers the valley floor. Only the narrow wagon
trail breaks the green monotony.
name: Serpentine Wagon Trail

desc:
As the trail brushes against the cliff walls to the east and the south, it bends
around them while changing direction from the north to the west. Water run-off
from above has caused the trail here to become soft and the ruts of wagon wheels
past can be seen. The washout from above has only allowed small grasses and
weeds to survive here.
name: Bend in the Rutted Wagon Trail

desc:
An old, worn trail passes through this region of the Tower Hills, continuing to
the north and west. Hills slope upward to the south and east, bordering the
trail. The steep hillside to the east is unstable, with scree and rubble resting
at its foot. Small ruts in the trail show where wagons have passed before. At
the narrowest point in the bend they are especially noticeable.
name: Bend in the Wagon Trail

desc:
A trail leads east and west, avoiding the steeper slopes of the surrounding
Tower Hills. The trail is just wide enough to allow wagons to move along it.
Weeds grow along the middle of the trail, but the majority of it is clear enough
to show that it is still used at least occasionally.
name: Straight Wagon Trail

desc:
Etched deeply into the Tower Hills this canyon must have a history all of its
own. The splintered rock formations provide cover for various lizards and mice
allowing them to escape from their larger predators. The canyon narrows as it
slopes down to the south with steep walls rising up on either side.
name: Descending Canyon

desc:
A steep incline awaits anyone trying to move forward from this canyon. Pebbles
have come to rest awkwardly along the canyon path here. The high rising canyon
walls choke off any passage to the east or west while to the north a short
scramble over rocks may be a way to leave the canyon. A gentle slope to the
south leads to the bottom of the canyon.
name: Descending Canyon

desc:
A ring of high hills encircles this lowland expanse. Several of the taller
hilltops have grass thickets growing along their ridgelines. Much of the
vegetation seems to have been trampled by large animals although little sign has
been left to show what species. A wide, worn path is visible to the west while
the Tower Hills extend to the north and south in a fairly straight line.
name: Tower Hills

desc:
As the hills continue to rise, the various peaks and valleys appear very
similar. The terrain, while not difficult to pass, could confuse a careless
traveller with the similarities in the landscape making navigation tricky. Large
hills dominate the landscape to the north and west but, in contrast, a sheer
drop extending from the east around to the south is impassable; the hills ending
abruptly in a steep canyon.
name: Tower Hills

desc:
The rolling, overgrown hills that dominate the surrounding areas appear to be
levelling out here. The heavily compacted earth means the landscape is barren,
devoid of almost all flora save for some fairly short grass and a few woody
shrubs. An unusual feature of this hill is the deep cleft that has opened up
leading steeply down into a canyon below. The exposed rock walls of the fissure
are jagged providing wide ledges that could be used as steps to reach the bottom
of the canyon far below.
name: Tower Hills

desc:
Rocks and debris fallen from the brittle walls litter the canyon floor. Barren
and destitute, the land here is too dry to support plant life. In its place a
thick blanket of dust covers the ground. A sheer wall on the southern edge of
the canyon blocks any hope of continuing in that direction. An ascending trail
can be seen to the north and the bottom of this particular canyon stretches out
to the east and west.
name: Bottom of a Canyon

desc:
Small pebbles lie scattered on the ground here at the lowest point of the
canyon. Dust clouds fly up when the wind blows and, in extreme cases, dust
devils send the smaller pieces of gravel scattering amongst the rocks. To the
north the canyon gives way to what appears to be an expanse of windswept fields,
to the west the canyon continues.
name: Bottom of a Canyon

desc:
Pieces of windblown grass litter the open grassy field. The grass grows high
enough to potentially hide predators within. The brush that makes up the fields
extends to the north and east but slowly thins and gives way to rocky ground far
to the south.
name: Windswept Field

desc:
The wild grass growing here butts up against the steep, craggy base of the Tower
Hills that dominate the land to the north and west. The thick, verdant grass has
scarcely been disturbed, indicating that there is not a significant quantity of
game in the area. To the south the grassy fields continue while to the east the
field appears to take on a rougher appearance.
name: Wild Field

desc:
The ground here is consists of rocks intermingled with small tufts of grass and
low-growing shrubs. Mottled hues spread over the ground; green where grass and
moss thrives and grey where stones, large and small, litter the ground. A
moss-covered granite crag rises to north, looking impassable to man or beast.
name: Rocky Grounds

desc:
These uncultivated fields are thick with wild, purple lupines and crisscrossed
with a few undulating streams. A small spring concealed by long grass and shrubs
bubbles up from the ground. Old, moss-covered stone walls are bordering the
fields, mostly intact even though they appear to have stood here for a very long
time.
name: Green Fields

desc:
Clusters of bushes and some trees spread on the predominantly flat ground. Among
the bushes, there are few open spaces, those that do exist are carpeted with
long grass. The ground here is dotted with huge boulders covered with thick,
green moss half embedded in the rich loam. Towards the east, the ground descends
down to the green meadows visible below.
name: Bushy Fields

desc:
A thick, dense ivy cloaks a small ruined cottage built from dry stone. Fallen
into disuse, the cottage is now slowly rotting away. Only parts of the crumbling
walls, an old door, and the fireplace remain of this derelict abode. The growth
of ivy is creeping up the ruined walls, covering them in a green blanket.
name: Ruined, Overgrown Cottage

desc:
Moss-covered peat blankets the soggy ground here. The wetland has a seemingly
simple flora; only patches of purple-coloured grass and long-stemmed bog cotton
grow on the wet, spongy ground. The small pools of water with their surrounding
tufts of bog grass give way to rockier terrain towards the north, where the land
rises.
name: Small Bog

desc:
A variety of colourful flowers thrives on a low-lying meadow. The dark green
grass grows long and lush in the rich soil with a plentiful supply of water.
Sedges and rushes form small stands in the damp depressions. Thick greyish
shrubs with small, round leaves grow taller than the surrounding undergrowth.
name: Green Fields

desc:
The grass here is thick and lush, growing in dense tussocks. An old ditch runs
along an expanding hedge, almost completely overgrown. The signs of cultivation
of the fields are slowly vanishing under the expanses of tall grass and shrub
thickets. Beside a withered oak tree, the grey fence poles of a sheep paddock
are still standing.
name: Field beside a Hedge

desc:
In a broad expanse of meadowland, splashes of colourful wildflowers punctuate a
sea of tall waving feathery grasses. A few mature individual trees grow on this
undulating grassland. In addition to the sparse tree cover, wild and untended
hedges block the view across an otherwise open landscape.
name: Lush Meadow Grass

desc:
Tall grass and a myriad of wild flowers grow on the open meadow. Only a few
short and shrub-like pine trees stand in the tall grass. This lively place seems
to attract a multitude of species of wildlife, providing food and shelter for
birds and small animals. Gently sloping hills to the west rise above the
surrounding flatlands.
name: Wildflower Meadow

desc:
A gentle rise ends abruptly at a steep escarpment. Boulders and scree lie around
the base of the slope having fallen from the cliff above. A few pine trees and
evergreen plants grow on the rocky ground. Some lichens grow hanging from tree
branches, resembling the grey hairs of an old man's beard. The jagged terrain to
the west looks difficult to reach.
name: Base of an Escarpment

desc:
Like gnarled fingers of a subterranean giant, a formation of large craggy rocks
protrudes from the grass. Standing alone on a featureless expanse with no other
stones or mountains in sight, these rock figures are unequivocally massive,
dwarfing even the tallest of men at their base. A mound of ash is here,
encircled by stones.
name: Rock Formation

desc:
The ground is covered by tall grasses and small bushes. Scattered stones and
rotting wood mark the remains of the fences that bordered what was once a
cultivated land. Steep hills rise in the west, continuing their ascent northward
where an escarpment can be seen in the distance. In the other directions, the
fields stretch for several miles.
name: Fields of Swaying Grass

desc:
The ground rises and falls in rolling, grass-covered hills. The hills are low
enough to be considered fields. The hilltops, if they could be called thus,
allows a slightly better view of the surrounding landscape. The grassy fields
extend for miles in all directions. Far to the west, and even further to the
north, a range of higher, rocky hills rises above the grasslands.
name: Fields of Swaying Grass

desc:
Man-high thistles tower over coach grass on this overgrown field. Remains of an
old stone wall can still be seen beside fields taken over by shrubs and weeds.
Some chiselled stones lie in the grass, perhaps marking a place where a barn or
a shelter once stood. A thick and prickly hedgerow creates an impenetrable
barrier to the north.
name: Overgrown Farmland

desc:
Tall, thick grass and weeds grow in abundance on this abandoned farmland. A huge
boulder, composed of weathered granite stands here. A clump of slender trees
surround the granite boulder, forming a small island on the field. Small
depressions remain where a ditch used run, long ago overgrown. Patches of
wildflowers border a faint trail leading to the north.
name: Huge Boulder on a Field

desc:
Several large hawthorn bushes grow in a cluster amidst a sea of swaying, green
grass. The tallest bush rises more than fifteen feet above the grass. Small
birds fly to and from their well-protected nests, which have been built deep
within the shrubbery. The grass-covered fields continue for miles in all
directions. Far to the west, a range of rocky hills can be seen.
name: Fields of Swaying Grass

desc:
A small brook flows into the fields from the hills in the north. The water
gathers into a small pool at the foot of the cliff before gently flowing away
across the lush grasslands in the south. A wide variety of plants and tall green
grasses flourish along the banks of the brook.
name: Field beside a Brook

desc:
A clear brook runs from the north to the south, its water slowly trickling
through the grasslands. The fields are overgrown with tall grass of a healthy
green colour, nourished by the fresh water of the stream. The streambed is
clearly visible through the water and the brook could easily be forded.
name: Along a Brook in the Grasslands

desc:
A small number of trees and some low bushes are the most easily distinguishable
features of the landscape's otherwise plain appearance. The ground in these
fields is mostly covered in tall and green grasses, gently swaying even in the
slightest breeze. Here and there amidst the soft soil, animal holes can be seen.
name: Fields of Swaying Grass

desc:
The rolling landscape of these vast fields is formed by many slightly sloping
low hills. Despite the sporadic clumps of tall grass, venturing here should
offer no more effort than the surrounding plains. The grass grows tall and green
on the southern sides of the hills, while the lower grass shares the northern
slopes with a large blanket of wet, green coloured moss.
name: Fields of Swaying Grass

desc:
The fields slope slightly up and down, yet still flat enough not to slow down
any venturer. The ground is covered in unusually tall, green grass and low
bushes. Sprawling north and south, the grasslands spread out in a vast blanket
of green, rolling and tumbling against the folds and furrows of the land. To the
west and east, lies an impenetrable conglomerate of thorny bushes and twisting
vines.
name: Fields of Swaying Grass

desc:
Both flowers and bushes alike prosper here. The soil is damp, yet hard to lay
eyes upon, as the vegetation grows remarkably dense. The grasslands continue in
all directions but east, where a rockier environment presents itself. A few
isolated trees grow in the meadow, offering a small shift in the backdrop.
name: Verdant Meadow

desc:
A dense forest in the southeast is the only distinctively different landmark
around, and without it the fields would seem to be endless in all directions.
Healthy grass grows thick on these gently sloping plains, providing fine
pastures for the indigenous animals to graze.
name: Verdant Meadow

desc:
Mixed grasses of different heights grow between small patches of yellow and red
star-shaped flowers. To the west, the sparkling surface of a stream can be seen
between the breaks in the emerald-green flora that thrives along the bank. The
ground is situated somewhat higher than the surrounding fields, allowing easy
survey of the grasslands that sprawl out for several miles in all directions.
name: Verdant Meadow

desc:
Making its way through the countryside, the brook flows southwards. Low bushes
grow on the banks of the brook, nourished by the clear, fresh water. Patches of
densely growing trees overshadow sections of the brook, offering protection for
both animal and traveller alike.
name: Along a Small Brook

desc:
Meandering in from the west, a lazy stream joins together with a small brook
that cuts through the grassland. Where the two converge, the water swirls and
bubbles, causing small funnels on the surface of the water. Tall reeds grow
along the banks, gently swaying back and forth.
name: Fork in the Brook

desc:
Tall grass dominates the landscape, gently bending at the slightest gust of
wind. The ground slopes sharply upwards towards the north, rising up to form an
overhanging cliff in the hills. Several boulders and small piles of rocks cover
the ground, probably having fallen from the northern inclination.
name: Grassland under a Cliff

desc:
The grasslands here are sparsely wooded with young trees and saplings. To the
west, the trees grow denser and taller as the fields transform into a proper
forest. Apart from the trees, the ground is mostly covered with low undergrowth;
small bushes and tall, green grass sprawl across the ground.
name: Field at the Edge of a Forest

desc:
A small stream emerges from the depths of a dense forest to the west. Although
littered with fallen leaves, twigs, and other forest debris, the water in the
creek largely appears to be clear and healthy. The vegetation is very sparse
outside the forest, consisting mainly of grass and saplings of the spreading
forest.
name: Stream Nearing a Forest

desc:
Large fir and pine trees grow increasingly sparse towards the east here, where
the forest slowly transitions into rolling grasslands. However less dense than
to the north and west, the canopy above is still thick enough to block any light
from reaching the forest floor. The ground is composed entirely of fallen pine
needles, twigs, and branches.
name: Edge of a Forest

desc:
Water flows in a small stream, running from east to west. The water meanders
around the trees and gathers in shallow pools where the ground is lower. The
water is shallow, and can easily be forded by foot or astride a mount. The trees
are a mix of mainly fir, pine, and birch, with a few hazel-trees scattered
amongst the forest. Most of the growth looks young, as the trees are little more
than saplings.
name: Stream at the Edge of a Forest

desc:
The forest grows thick and dense, making it hard to venture any other way than
south. The forest seems to be young, no more than a few decades at most.
Saplings and small trees grow in impregnable thickets and the forest floor is
covered with bushes and plants. The ground, where visible, seems damp and the
earthy odour of wet soil fills the air.
name: Damp Forest

desc:
A thin fog-like haze hangs suspended above the forest floor. The air is damp and
the ground wet. This forest is young, overgrown with saplings and bushes, with
no towering ancients in sight. Steep cliffs rise to the north and west, casting
parts of the forest in murky shadows.
name: Damp Forest

desc:
Seeping from the forest floor here, a forest spring submerges most of the
low-laying ground in a few inches of water. Constantly meandering left and
right, a murky stream slowly runs towards the east. This spring is unmistakably
the source of the surrounding forest's sogginess.
name: Forest Spring

desc:
Deep puddles of water form still, leaf-covered pools between groves of saplings
and sprawling bushes. The northern part of this forest has a particularly moist
forest floor. The lower trunks of the trees and the drier parts of the ground
are covered in different types of mosses, all thriving in the soggy forest.
name: Damp Forest

desc:
Sapling pines and firs make up most of the young forest here. The trees grow
dense and the needle-covered branches intersect above to create a green canopy.
The ground is covered with grass, moss and ferns. The sound of running water can
be heard in the distance, and to the south, a large tower is visible through the
canopy.
name: Edge of a Forest

desc:
A tall white tower rises gracefully from atop the Tower Hills in the southwest.
Every sunset the tower is bound to cast its shadow for miles across these
fields. Where it stands on a steep hill, the tower can not be directly
approached from here. The grass covered fields extend to the north, east, and
south while they give way to a dense forest to the west.
name: Fields below a White Tower

desc:
Low bushes thrive in the fertile, wet soil here. Flowing lazily through the
grassland towards the south, the water of a stream bubbles and clucks as it
flows over barely submerged rocks. The streambed is clearly visible, consisting
mainly of sand and rocks, worn smooth after years in the water.
name: Brook in the Tower Hills

desc:
Lush grasslands stretch out far, save for a forest miles to the southeast. This
particular field is completely devoid of any plants higher than a man's waist. A
tall, splintered stump stands upon a small grassy mound, next to which lies a
massive oak tree. Even in death, the branches spread magnificently, save for
many that are charred and blackened, evident of a mighty lightning strike years
ago.
name: Fallen Tree upon a Field

desc:
The plains here are covered in a dense layer of healthy green grass, with the
occasional small mound of flowers and broad-leaved ferns. Gazing south, a fenced
pasture can be seen in the distance, with a small farm house standing close by.
name: Field by a Pasture

desc:
The landscape descends into a deep valley. Bordering a low, dense forest in the
south, some patches of trees have rooted in the valley. The ground is rich with
grass, evergreen bushes, and plenty of saplings. To the east, the trees grow
more plentiful as the field transitions to forest.
name: Valley outside a Forest

desc:
Soggy, waterlogged ground surrounds the tall beech trees that grow in the boggy
peat. The fertile land has meant that the forest, which appears to have escaped
the lumberjack's axe for many years, has grown lush and dense. Layers of moss
cover the thick roots that snake between the trees, seeking out nutrients in the
damp ground. To the north and west, the soggy ground becomes an impassable mire,
beyond which the ground becomes drier as it climbs in elevation.
name: Damp Forest

desc:
The soil is soft and moist here supporting vegetation that is predominantly made
up by moss, low bushes, and ferns. Firs and pines grow straight and tall here,
the lower part of their trunks completely devoid of the needle-like leaves that
coniferous trees usually carry. The trees are spaced well apart from each other,
allowing the ground vegetation to thrive.
name: Damp Forest

desc:
Large numbers of fir and pine saplings grow here, their thin trunks reaching
towards the sky. A tall white tower can be sighted through the forest's canopy.
Looming over the forest, the tower stands on a steep hill to the east. At the
base of the hill, a small cave entrance is just barely visible behind branches,
trunks, and the needlelike leaves of the trees.
name: Depths of a Damp Forest

desc:
The walls of this cave are uneven and rough. The floor at the southern end of
the cavern is covered in a layer of grass and leaves. A low and narrow opening
in the western wall leads to a forest outside the cave. Along the inner wall to
the east, there is a section that looks noticably smoother than the surrounding
rock, around which small strands of spider silk gently sway as if pushed by an
unseen breeze.
name: Small Cave

desc:
Firs and pine trees, amongst other types of conifers grow dense here. The canopy
is thick, allowing only just enough light for the moss that grows on the lower
parts of the tree trunks to thrive. Steep hills rise in all directions but
north, where the forest continues its motionless march onward.
name: Depths of a Damp Forest

desc:
Rising towards the sky, the tower on the western hill overlooks this entire
region. Some of the windows of the tower are facing this direction. Without
doubt, a fall from there would be absolutely fatal. The landscape east of the
hills is flourishing with grass and various meadow flowers, such as daisies and
red poppy.
name: Grasslands below the Tower Hills

desc:
The fields are covered with grass, weeds, and low bushes. To the west, steep
hillsides block travel as the land rises and becomes more arid. To the north,
east, and south, the grassy expanse continues, only interupted by a small creek
to the east.
name: Lush Grasslands

desc:
Seemingly impenetrable hills rise in the west, graced by an impressive tower on
the peak in the far north. Soft and moist, the soil here is kept fertile by a
nearby creek that flows towards the east. Various kinds of flowers cover the
grasslands, among them oxeye daisy and cornflower.
name: Lush Grasslands

desc:
The grass that covers the land here is both thick and healthy, due to the nearby
creek that flows freely towards the east. The grasslands slope slightly upwards
to the west, while eastwards it descends into the small creek. Gazing to the
southeast, a faint trail can be spotted amongst patches of tall, green grass.
name: Lush Grasslands

desc:
A sparkling brook flows in from the elevated northern grasslands. The stream's
water is crystal clear, maybe suggesting that it is safe to drink. Small
pebbles, rounded by countless years in the flowing water, occupy the streambed
Various kinds of grasses and flowers are thriving here in the damp soil. To the
east, a wide, wooden plank spans across the water, where it meets a faint path
that meanders through the grass towards a small farmhouse.
name: Brook by a Farm

desc:
A massive carpet of grass and low bushes covers the landscape. Nourished by the
brook that flows through here, the grass looks vigorous and in perpetual growth.
The stream looks very fresh, and would satisfy the needs of traveller, animal,
and plant alike. A dense forest is distinguishable far to the south-east.
name: Brook in the Grasslands

desc:
A fresh brook flows through the fertile grasslands. The water's flow is slow and
lazy, and the streambed is clearly visible beneath the stream's surface. The
plains are lush with healthy, green grasses. An assortment of orange, yellow,
and red flowers sprout up in patches amongst the grass, dotting the landscape in
colourful patterns.
name: Brook in the Grasslands

desc:
The grasslands descend steeply from both sides into a shallow brook. Aided both
by fertilisation brought by the animals that come to the creek to drink and the
clear water of the stream itself, the grass grows very lush and strong here.
name: Brook in the Grasslands

desc:
The forest floor is wet and muddy in the west, however towards the east, the
ground becomes drier. Trees of many kinds, such as birch, elm, and hazel are
thriving here, nourished by the fertile and humid soil. The trees are fairly
dense, and although not at all impossible, care should be had when moving
through the forest.
name: Outskirts of a Forest

desc:
The forest here is densely populated by young trees, their thin trunks bending
at the slightest gust of wind. The ground is damp and covered with brown leaves
shed from the surrounding birch, beech, and hazels from autumns past. The trees
seem to grow both higher and thicker in the east, indicating that this part of
the forest is older.
name: Dense Forest

desc:
The trees here are covered in a thick, dark lichen. Birch, hazel, and beech
stand tall and proud, suggesting this is an older part of the otherwise
young-in-appearance forest. The ground is covered in a thick blanket of rotting
foliage. A few gnarled roots protrude from the leaves and snake across the
ground, before returning back into the earth in search of nutrients.
name: Heart of a Forest

desc:
Hazel and beech are all the trees that seem to reside in this part of the dense
forest. All the trunks are slender, swaying gently in the slightest gust of
wind. The forest opens up into grass-covered fields to the north. In the south
the trees grow taller and wider. Some trees here seem to have scratch marks on
the bottom of their trunks.
name: Edge of a Forest

desc:
This woodland area is densely populated with hazel, birch, and beech. All of
them several years from their utmost height and their ultimate width. The forest
floor is soft, delicately packed with fallen leaves of the last autumn. To the
west, a long, impassable wall of fallen and uprooted trees stretches into the
distance.
name: Dense Forest

desc:
Bordering a dense forest to the east, the grasslands here are to some extent
covered with trees. Birch and hazel are among the trees trying to recapture the
grassy plains and to turn it into forest again. The ground is soft and muddy,
permeated by the creek that flows freely to the west. A small farmhouse is
visible in the north.
name: Grasslands near a Forest

desc:
Even for a house in the peaceful countryside, this would be considered a very
humble residence. The walls are made from cut lengths of cedar, which lends a
sweet smelling aroma to the room. There is no ceiling to this single-room
structure, only thick, wooden rafters stand between the floor and the pinnacle
of the roof. Upon the southern wall, there is a large mahogany mural of a pony.
Several thin ropes and a leather saddle hang from a peg near the corner. From
the window to the east, a fenced pasture can be seen sprawling across the land.
name: Home of a Pony Breeder

desc:
Enclosed by fences, these grasslands have been turned into a pasture for
livestock. The grass is kept permanently short by the ponies that are bred here.
Alongside the southern edge of the field, a dense forest grows, its lowest
branches growing over the fence to the pasture.
name: Fenced Pasture

desc:
Somewhat elevated, the landscape overlooks a distant trail that can be seen
through the trees to the south and east. The woodlands here are composed mainly
of birch and beech, their trunks pale and smooth against the dark backdrop of
the surrounding forest. A thick blanket of leaves lie in perpetual decay upon
the uneven ground.
name: Elevated Forest

desc:
The forest shifts as the trail bends to the south and the east. While the forest
grows sparse in the south, the trees on the northern side of the trail grow not
only high, but also dense enough to prevent any detectable access to those parts
of the forest.
name: Faded Trail through a Forest

desc:
This woodland area is densely populated with young trees; a mixture of beech,
hazel, and birch compete skyward for the most sun. The undergrowth is littered
with old leaves, but occasionally a small tree or a bush finds a spot with
enough light to thrive. The west-facing sides of the trunks are covered in a
thick coat of lichen.
name: Dense Forest

desc:
A hill rises in the northwest, its steepness denying further venture of the
forest in that direction. Several of the trees here carry scratch marks on the
bases of their trunks. To the north, barely discernible behind some sprawling
shrubbery, is a gaping hole at the bottom of a natural embankment. Small tufts
of grey and white fur hang from the branches of the shrubs near the opening.
name: Woodland outside a Den

desc:
The forest here is low and very dense. The local fauna consists of hazels,
birches, beeches, and elms, none even close to their full height or width. A
hill rises in the northwest, covered in dense forest. The forest floor mainly
exhibits low bushes and a soft bed of old, rotting leaves.
name: Young Forest

desc:
The trees here grow close to each other and straight upwards, competing for the
obtainable sunlight. Covering the forest floor are many thick, moss-covered
roots, which seem to dive in and out of the rich soil below. Upon the forest
floor lies a thick layer of dead leaves, twigs, and branches.
name: Edge of a Forest

desc:
The trail leads through a dense forest. None at full height, thin hazels and
beeches compete for an unrivalled chance of sunlight. A thick layer of fallen
leaves are decaying on the ground, providing nourishment for the herbivores that
forage here.
name: Faded Trail

desc:
A thin forest surrounds the trail on the north and south sides. The trail is
partly overgrown with tree sprouts and mosses, suggesting that visitors are a
rare sight in these forests. The trees to the north are noticeably higher than
those that grow nearby.
name: Faded Trail through a Forest

desc:
The trail cuts through the young yet dense forest. Hazel, beech, and elm grow
tall in the west. Towards the east and south, the vegetation is lower and denser
and the trees are surrounded by low bushes. The trail is barely visible beneath
the fallen leaves. Many years of neglect have slowly erased the boundary between
forest and trail.
name: Faded Trail

desc:
Dominated by beech, elm, and birch, the forest here extends in all directions.
Overhead, thick tree branches grow rather dense, competing with each other for
available sunlight. The trail here is barely discernible beneath the fallen
leaves, twigs, and branches.
name: Faded Trail through a Forest

desc:
Judging by their appearance, these barren fields have been abandoned for a long
time. The fertile top layers of soil have blown away, leaving nothing but dust
and stone. Many of the larger rocks are blackened at their highest points, as if
they were burned when a fire swept across this land long ago.
name: Barren Fields

desc:
This path is dusty and windswept. It follows the edge of a large field to the
west. Dry, withered weeds lie upon the edges of the path, continuing further out
into the surrounding fields. Several lengths of cracked and blackened poles
stand between the field and the path, resembling what was once a proud, sturdy
fence. The path continues to the north, into what looks like the ruins of a
small village.
name: Path through Barren Fields

desc:
A path from the south ends at a small open area here. The open area seems likely
to be the village square. Old, partially-collapsed buildings surround the
square. The walls that still stand look blackened, as if a fire burned the
village down sometime long ago. The ground is dry, dusty, and mostly barren.
name: Path through a Ruined Village

desc:
These fields are split into large squares with paths between them. The soil is a
burnt-clay red, stony, and mostly barren. Nothing but a few struggling weeds
grow here, barely clinging to life. The lifeless fields continue to the north
and east. The edge of a forest is visible far to the south, beyond a valley.
name: Barren Fields

desc:
These fields have a blackened, unhealthy look. A fire may be the cause of the
devastation, but if so it happened long ago. Some weeds fight an uneven battle
for survival on the otherwise lifeless field. For weeds, they look remarkably
miserable. The barren fields continue to the east and south, while the partially
collapsed wall of a house blocks passage north. To the west, the ground is
covered with thick, thorny shrubbery that clings to the rapidly ascending
hillside, making travel in that direction hard.
name: Ruined Fields

desc:
Here, the grasslands start to mix with a dense forest to the east. Towards the
west, the land slopes considerably, before meeting the clear waters of a small
creek. To the south, a partially overgrown trail is barely visible between tall
tufts of grass, which predominantly covers the landscape.
name: Grasslands near a Forest

desc:
From atop these hills, the emerald-green planes of rolling grasslands stretch on
for miles. Towards the north, the top of a tower white stands out against the
skyline. The hills are dotted with small, star-shaped flowers that lend a
vibrant contrast to the surrounding sea of green that sprawls out to the east.
In all other directions, the land becomes rocky and arid, as towering rock
formations thrust upwards towards the sky.
name: Twin Hills

desc:
A trail bends sharply to the south and east here. Overgrown with grass and
bushes, it is not obvious how far it continues straight in any direction. In the
east, the land appears to be cut by a valley running north and south. Far north,
the top of a tower presents itself.
name: Bend in a Faded Trail

desc:
A clear brook intersects an overgrown trail here, where the disintegrating
remains of what appears to have been an old wooden bridge lies half sunken in
the muddy streambed. Beneath the surface of the water, the beams once used to
support the bridge can be seen, upon which several lengths of green beard lichen
drift lazily in the mild current.
name: Trail and Brook Crossroads

desc:
Flanked by the lush grasses of the plains, the trail is easily discernible in
the distance to the east, where a treeline begins to form. To the west, the
trail meanders through the vegetation at a gradual descent, until finally
intersecting a burbling creek. Upon the ground here, small bushes and twigs
intermingle with the grasses.
name: Faded Trail

desc:
In the north and in the east, a light forest grows next to the trail. The trees
at the outskirts of the forest grow in sparse formations of low height,
indicating that the forest is both young and expanding. To the west, the trail
cuts straight through the lush grasslands seemingly for several miles.
name: Faded Trail at the Edge of a Forest

desc:
A thin forest surrounds the trail on the east and south sides. The trail is
partly overgrown with tree sprouts and mosses, suggesting that visitors are a
rare sight in these forests. The trees in the north are noticeably higher than
those in the other directions, while still not reaching their full height.
name: Faded Trail through a Forest

desc:
Towards the north and west, a faint trail can be seen weaving between the
lichen-covered trunks of towering trees. The low hanging branches are completely
devoid of foliage, either from lack of sunlight or the voracious appetites of
the local fauna. The ground is covered with clumps of stunted grass and bushes.
name: Edge of a Forest

desc:
Tall grass grows in abundance across these sprawling fields. Moderately uniform
in height, various types of weeds, thistles, and bushes thrive here, offering
magnificent cover for hiding animals. A low forest flourishes in the northwest,
stretching for several miles. Several animal paths cross the grasslands,
emerging from the forest's edge.
name: Bushy Grassland

desc:
The forest here bears several markings of animal activity. Most of the trees
have noticeable claw marks on their trunks. A hole in the ground is visible in a
hill in the south, where many tracks can be seen leading to and from the
opening. Towards the east, the trees taper off into a bushy grassland.
name: Outskirts of a Forest

desc:
Dug out from the soft soil, this oval cave is undoubtedly the work of some
animal. Roots of various lengths and sizes dangle from the ceiling, searching in
vain for nutrients. Along the walls of the den, deep claw marks have been
scraped into the dirt. Small tufts of silvery hair lie upon the ground next to a
gnawed piece of wood.
name: Small Den

desc:
A low and wet grassland expands for miles here, the water-lodged ground hosting
a wide variety of plants and grasses. A slow stream flows in from the north, and
disappears to the southeast. To the east, a forest-like shape gives a hint of
somewhat higher ground compared to the immediate surroundings.
name: Flooded Plains

desc:
A fading trail comes in from the west and leads northwards. The trail crosses an
area of wide plains here. The plains slope gently downward to the east, and as
the ground gets lower the ground looks water-logged. The vegetation change from
grass and shrubs to an expansive growth of long reedmace, their heads looking
like cat tails sticking out of the mud.
name: Faded Trail

desc:
The faded trail crosses grass-covered fields, leading east and west. Another
path, carefully laid with cobblestones, but overgrown with lichen and seemingly
disused, leads north. Lush grasslands expands for miles to the south; there is
no end to them in sight.
name: Faded Trail

desc:
The path leading from the south makes a sudden turn westward here, effectively
avoiding a vertical drop into the northern and eastern plains. Boulders of
various sizes have been placed at even intervals between the edge of the path
and the precipitous sheer; to serve a warning, or simply provide a place to sit
and view the landscape, the purpose of the rocks is unknown. Thick layers of
lichen cling to the cobblestone, lending a greenish-brown stain to the surface
of the path.
name: The Tower Path

desc:
An old, dust-covered trail leads off to the east and north. To the south, at the
foot of a steep cliff, another trail follows the fields at the foot of the
hills. The two trails seem to converge to the south-east. Towards the north a
tower can barely be sighted. The road is set with stones with vegetation
springing up between the cracks where they meet.
name: The Tower Path

desc:
Atop these nearly-barren hills, an old, dusty path leads to a tall white tower
that rises to the north. Small patches of grass and a few low bushes constitute
the only flora here. Dry twigs and natural debris lie scattered on the ground,
caught in various states of decay. To the east, two perky grass-covered hills
can be seen rising in the distance, each crowned with a circular bed of vibrant
flowers. To the north and south, the path continues onward.
name: The Tower Path

desc:
A dusty path follows the ridge of the hill as it bends to the east and south
here. At the bottom of the steep northern hillside a dense forest covers the
plains for several miles. To the north-east the path seems to lead to a
grandiose white tower. Small bushes and wild grass line the path.
name: The Tower Path

desc:
An old cobblestone path makes its way through the Tower Hills, making a sharp
turn from the west to the north to avoid some rugged landscape. The stones that
were once set with great precision and care, now reflect the passing of time and
the elements. Lichen has stained the path a ruddy orange, and several weeds have
grown up between the stones.
name: The Tower Path

desc:
A large tower rises towards the sky to the north. It is made out of large white
and grey stones, giving it an almost glittering quality when the light refracts
off the smooth surfaces. A large, sturdy door of oak is set within the southern
wall of the tower. An old cobblestone path, thick with vegetation sprouting
between the gaps in the stones, leads from the tower through the hills to the
south.
name: Before a White Tower

desc:
Overgrown vegetation reaching several feet high grows here. The plants are of
dark green colour and are remarkably untouched by grazing wild life. A bit to
the west, the grasslands transform into a flowery bed. A faint trail that might
carry travellers makes it way through the green lands to the north.
name: Lush Plains

desc:
Judging from the vegetation and lower ground continuing to the north, a stream
might have flowed through the plains a long time ago. A few long plants that
belong to a body of water give hints about seasonal changes that might resurrect
the stream. To the west, elevated plains bear comparatively lusher vegetation.
name: Lower Plains

desc:
The air is full of sweet scents and vivid colours fill this flowery meadow. The
surrounding grasslands look drab and colourless in comparison to this area. A
faint trail can be seen crossing the grasslands to the north, running from west
to east.
name: Flowery Meadow

desc:
These plains are several feet higher than the surrounding landscape. A tree
grows on the edge of this elevated grassland. Beneath the tree, there are signs
of a well-frequented campsite. The nearby grass has been sat on and trampled by
animals. Colourful spots in the plains to the north hint that many flowers grow
there.
name: Higher Plains

desc:
These otherwise flat and uniform plains are punctuated by a few scattered
bushes. Many green plants that reach several feet high grow on the land. The
flora isn't very colourful, being mostly green with a few yellow flowers peeking
through.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
Green vegetation continues in every direction as far as the eye can see. The
otherwise homogenous landscape is broken up by patches of red and blue. To the
south, the plains drop a few feet to softer terrain. Fields to the west give a
scene of another descent in the nearby grasslands.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
The land rises to the north and falls to the south. This landscape marks the
last vestiges of the sloping plains which lie to the west. One cannot see much
over the slope to the north, but the sight to south-east verifies that the
surrounding plains are rich in plant life and could be a sanctuary to all sorts
of animals.
name: Sloping Plains

desc:
A field with mostly blue, red and yellow wildflowers grows in soil that would
hinder any passing traveller. The vegetation seems to love the fertility,
stretching close to two feet in height. To the north and west, equally vibrant
fields give a sense that this is the typical landscape for miles and miles.
name: Lush Plains

desc:
A very flat and lush land, covered with wildflowers and grasses, extends for
miles. The thickness and height of vegetation give reason to believe that the
soil in which it grows is very fertile. The same fertility seems to continue in
the plains to the east and a valley to the west. A land of dense bushes opens up
southwards, stretching for many miles.
name: Fertile Plains

desc:
A wide vista opens up from here. Flowery grasslands lie in the east, lush
grasslands stretch to the south and trail runs through the plains to the north
and west. A few dense bushes stretch their branches over the grass and could
provide shade on a sunny day. Near an especially dense and tall bush, there are
footprints made by sandals or shoes, and a heap of charcoal from a campfire.
name: Plains beside the Faded Trail

desc:
A faint trail runs east and west through these grasslands. A well-travelled path
can be spotted to the north atop a steep cliff. Scattered bushes are visible in
the grassy plains which lie to the south. The bushes have missing foliage,
suggesting either that they are suffering from some sort of illness or that some
beast has been eating their leaves.
name: Faded Trail

desc:
The corner of a faint trail continues through the grass to the east and south.
The trail sometimes disappears and is indistinguishable from the rest of the
plains, making it hard to follow and difficult to work out how frequently it is
used. A steep hillside lies to the north, blocking any traveller from climbing.
name: Faded Trail

desc:
A route through the grasslands seems to continue to the north-east and
south-west directions. The sounds of chirping birds fill the immediate vicinity.
A few of the birds fly in and out of the many small bushes, then back to the
sky. On the trail, there seems to have been recent activity as indicated by
footprints and animal tracks. A magnificent sight of colourful vegetation and
bushes opens up to the south.
name: Faded Trail

desc:
A narrow dirt track runs through a sea of low grass. It looks like the trail was
once wider and more used, but the vegetation is encroaching on both sides of the
path, threatening to overgrow it entirely. Despite its proximity to the hills
west of here, the land itself is largely flat, rising and dipping only a few
feet at a time.
name: Faded Trail

desc:
This trail runs through a broad meadow. Different types of grass vie with one
another for dominance, some low and coarse, others reaching two feet or more in
height. A small sprinkling of white and pale pink flowers add colour to the
landscape. The trail is hard-beaten earth in most places, thought in a few it is
barely visible beneath the encroaching grass.
name: Faded Trail

desc:
A faint dirt track twists back and forth through a series of very low hillocks,
extending generally from southwest to northeast. The ground is studded with
small rocks and the soil is sandy and poor. Some short, thin grass grows in
scattered clumps, but the majority of the plantlife here is thistles and other
weeds.
name: Faded Trail

desc:
This narrow dirt path appears to be old and little used, but it is still quite
serviceable for the time being. Grass and weeds are slowly pushing their way
across the path, held at bay only by the hard-packed surface. A handful of
stunted trees grow to the north, while south of here the ground drops away very
suddenly into a green valley.
name: Faded Trail

desc:
The trail makes a sharp turn here, extending to the east and south. A number of
bushes run beside the northwestern side of the trail, becoming denser farther
from the trail. On the other side, sickly grass competes with sturdy weeds in
the poor soil. The path is not difficult to follow, but it looks like it is not
as heavily travelled now as it might once have been.
name: Faded Trail

desc:
The trail runs close to the edge of a cliff to the east, beyond which a wide
green valley extends for miles. The track is poorly defined at some points as
tall, thick grass fights to reclaim it, but for now at least it is easy enough
to follow. At a few points, the trail vanishes entirely for several feet as it
crosses bare grey stone.
name: Faded Trail

desc:
Running almost straight from east to west, the trail rises and falls as it
crosses folds in the terrain. At times it runs through grass, but just as often
it passes across bare rock and becomes very difficult to see until it reenters
the vegetation. Scattered bushes stand beside the north side of the trail, as
does a clump of a half-dozen maples.
name: Faded Trail

desc:
Hills rise to both the north and south, leaving only a narrow stretch of lower
terrain between them. The ground is strewn with countless rocks of every size
and description, deposited here by ages of erosion. A very faint trail picks its
way through the debris, nearly vanishing in spots as it crosses flat stone. Just
to the east, the hills are lower and far greener.
name: Faint Trail

desc:
A faint path winds around large boulders and dodges gullies and hillsides,
maintaining as level a course as it can through the hills. At times the trail is
difficult to see, but generally reappears within five or ten yards. There is
little to see here in the way of vegetation, but to the north the hills become
much greener.
name: Faded Trail

desc:
This trail appears to get rather little use, although that can be difficult to
judge in areas where it crosses bare rock. Small tufts of grass and scattered
weeds nestle between the stones and boulders that lie to either side of the
path. A plateau rises to the south and east. The slope to the east is far too
steep and bare to scale. The hillside to the south is much gentler, but will
still require some skill and effort to ascend.
name: Faded Trail

desc:
While generally running due east and west, the trail here has to snake its way
back and forth around enormous outcroppings of rock. The stone is banded in
layers of orange, brown, and grey, along with a few very thin bands of black.
Pale green and white lichen clings to many of the exposed surfaces, while dark
moss hides in the shadows.
name: Winding Faded Trail

desc:
Although not tall, this hill has steep, uneven slopes that can make travel
difficult. None is steeper than the eastern slope, which is a nearly vertical
cliff, dropping away into a lower, flatter area. Grass covers most of the hill,
except where patches of the underlying stone push their way up above the soil. A
few scattered oaks and maples dot the hillsides.
name: Low Green Hill

desc:
The hills are very low here, vanishing entirely after a short cliff just to the
east. Short, thick grass covers the hillsides, interspersed with wildflowers in
an array of pale blues, pinks, and violets. Several isolated elms, oaks, and
alders rise high above the land, casting small islands of shades. The hills
continue in every direction but to the east.
name: Edge of the Green Hills

desc:
Numerous pebbles along with missing leaves from nearby bushes suggest that a
local animal track must be close by. Landscapes to the north and west betray a
hidden path slithering through the plains. Putting the two together, this path
might be a way for hunters to detect and catch wild game that rests on these
lands.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
These wet grasslands are densely covered in bushes, hindering the passage of
travellers. A variety of plants grow in scattered pools of water that lie
between bushes. Some of the leaves of bushes and plants are missing, indicating
some sort of animal presence. To the south, the density of bushes increases,
while to the west a land with only grass fills the view.
name: Bushy Plains

desc:
A field of bushes, through which travelling might be a troublesome act, continue
the pattern of lowlands. The land is wet and even covered with shallow puddles,
hinting at frequent rainfall and bushes with lots of foliage providing shade
from the sun. There is a strange mixture of plants at the pools of water. To the
west, a pond can be spotted in the middle of a valley. To the north, the
lowlands continue but the density of bushes seems to decrease.
name: Bushy Plains

desc:
A very large pond is nestled in the valley, hundreds of yards across in every
direction. The water is mostly clear and its bottom sandy, where not covered in
thick green weeds. Tiny minnows flash and dart in every direction, while larger
fish can sometimes be spotted nosing their way through the vegetation. A few
tall willow and elm trees stand scattered around the bank, offering both shade
and prime fishing spots.
name: Pond in the Lush Valley

desc:
The land slopes downward as it progresses toward the south, where it opens up
into a large green valley. Millions of small stones and tiny chips of rock are
embedded in the soil here, creating alternating patches of bare earth and thick
weeds. Some small bushes are scattered around the area, but nothing manages to
grow taller than a few feet.
name: Sloping Plains

desc:
The land dips down from the north into a large, fertile valley that extends for
miles. The quality of the soil here is clearly demonstrated by the thick growth
that covers the landscape. Dark-green grass dominates the area, but thousands of
wildflowers thrust up above the grass in clusters of colour that span the full
range of the spectrum from white to deep purple.
name: Fertile Valley

desc:
Low but dense grass fills this portion of the valley, rising only a few inches
above the soil but creating a thick, cushioned carpet. In the few places where
the soil can be seen, it is nearly black. Some of the grass has been cropped off
close to the ground, indicating some grazing animals must pass through here from
time to time. The greenery extends in every direction, but is broken up to the
south by a large pond.
name: Lush Valley

desc:
Thick greenery extends on every side. In many places, it is broad-bladed grass
of medium height, rising a foot or two above the ground. However, there are also
several small cultivated fields here planted with wheat, oats, barley, and
various other crops. A narrow line of trampled grass leads west, not broad
enough to be a true trail but clearly often used, while a large pond can be seen
to the east.
name: Lush Valley

desc:
This valley extends for miles in every direction but west, where it ends quite
suddenly at a tall cliff. Thick green grass rises two to three feet here in many
spots, but in several places wide meadows have been flattened by swirling winds,
creating intricate patterns of grass crushed low to the ground. Half a dozen
short maples rise above the fields, standing as isolated islands in a sea of
green.
name: Lush Valley

desc:
Thick emerald grass fills the floor of the valley here, growing as much as three
feet high in some spots. The blades dip and sway in even the slightest wind, as
if dancing to some unheard musicians. Only a few tall wildflowers manage to rise
above the grass, their white and yellow blossoms showing up in sharp contrast to
the green. Sheer cliffs border the valley to the north and west.
name: Edge of a Lush Valley

desc:
This valley is bordered on the west and south by high cliffs of dark grey stone.
The valley extends to the north, growing narrower as it goes. Next to the cliffs
there are low mounds of boulders and scree, with very little growing there other
than a little moss. Further out, grass dominates the floor of the valley. A
faint path leads north to a small village.
name: Deep Valley

desc:
Too narrow and faint to be a true road, this trail leads its way through the
valley floor and runs north into a very small village. The trail is bordered on
both sides by white stones, and a multicoloured row of flowers has been planted
along the western edge as well. A small but carefully constructed wooden house
stands a few yards off the trail to the east.
name: Trail through a Village

desc:
A narrow trail runs in from the south and forms into a broad loop here in the
centre of a very small village. There are only a few houses in the entire
village, along with a single barn that stands to the north. The largest of the
houses stands to the west. A single tall elm dominates the centre of the area,
with a couple of narrow benches set up in its shade.
name: Centre of a Village

desc:
This large barn is entirely open on its southern side, with a large doorway but
no doors. Stalls line the eastern wall of the building, while a pile of hay that
rises more than ten feet high lines most of the western wall, leaving only
enough space for one rack of farming tools and another with tack for horses.
This barn seems to serve the needs of the whole community.
name: Open Barn

desc:
This level expanse is covered almost entirely in grass. Most of the growth is
only six to eight inches high, but a different species of taller blades grows
scattered among the rest, reaching two feet or more in height. In a few spots
patches of grey stone thrust their way up through the growth. To the east and
south, the plateau drops off into a deep green valley.
name: Grassy Plateau

desc:
This ridgeline flattens out as it extends to the north, while narrowing to the
south. Thick grass covers a large portion of the ridge, although in many places
brown and orange rock is visible where there is no soil. A scattering of pale
blue and purple flowers add a splash of variety to the greenery. A tall plateau
rises to the west in a sheer cliff of dark grey stone.
name: Grass-covered Ridge

desc:
These hills rise and fall without pattern, creating a maze of valleys between
them. Weeds and shrubs have taken root in the lower reaches of the hillsides,
but most of the hilltops are bare rock, sporting only small tufts of grass and a
few stunted trees with tangled roots clingig to the stone. The trees grow taller
and become more common to both the west and north.
name: Rolling Hills

desc:
Firs, aspens, and a few yews dot the hilltop, rising high above the short, dark
grass that covers most of the area. In their shade there is less growth, but
abundant piles of dead branches, leaves, and needles that might provide shelter
for many small animals. To the south and east, the hill drops away sharply in a
hundred narrow channels eroded into the rock, giving it the appearance of a
pleated green dress.
name: Wooded Cliff

desc:
Enormous cracks riddle the hills, exposing layers of folded rock in a myriad of
colours. The rock in the cracks is much less weathered than the rest of the
hills. While many of the crevices are deep, most are narrow and do not extend
very far in any direction, so it is not difficult to avoid them. Little grows on
the hills, leaving them dull grey and brown.
name: Broken Hills

desc:
The ground has been buckled by earthquakes or some other incredible force,
creating sharp folds. Shattered remains of enormous boulders litter the ground,
their edges dulled by ages of rain and wind. While rocks dominate the broken
landscape, a large number of durable weeds have taken hold in the scant soil,
producing patches of green amid the brown and grey stone.
name: Broken Hills

desc:
This hilltop rises higher than most, even among the Tower Hills. While it is
possible to see a great distance in any direction from here, the folded and
broken nature of the surrounding hills make it impossible to use this spot as an
effective vantage point. The hill drops slightly and flattens out to the south,
where an area dominated by grass rather than rocks is visible.
name: Top of a High Hill

desc:
The hills here are nestled against a tall ridgeline that borders them to both
the north and east. Steep, barren slopes rise to meet the ridge, but while they
are not vertical, they are too smooth to allow an ascent. The bases of the
slopes are heaped with scree and boulders that have broken loose from the ridge
and come to rest here.
name: Hills below a Ridge

desc:
Deep folds in the ground create sharp ridgelines of bare rock and shadowy
valleys filled with twisted shrubs and sparse but rugged grass. Ages of fallen
leaves and dead grass decay on the ground, producing more fertile soil than is
commonly found in this area. A taller hill dominates the view to the south.
name: Tower Hills

desc:
A barren cliff rises to the east until it becomes a tall ridgeline, standing
sentinel over the Tower Hills. The cliff blocks any sunlight from ever reaching
this place before noon, and the sparse vegetation that does survive here tends
to grow on the western-facing hillsides. In the shadows, pale green and white
lichen clings to the rocks.
name: Below a Cliff

desc:
A tall ridge rises to the east and north, its sides too steep to ascend. Loose
scree covers the base of the ridge, broken fragments of stone fallen over the
ages from above. A few wildflowers spring up from cracks in the rocky ground,
fighting for nourishment and adding a splash of colour to an otherwise mostly
grey landscape.
name: Hills below a Ridge

desc:
A narrow ridgeline begins here, rising as it proceeds further to the north. The
slopes on either side are not too steep at the base, but they become far more
vertical as the ridge rises. The eastern slope of the ridge is much gentler and
a layer of soil has managed to accumulate there, providing a foothold for thin,
pale grass. The steeper western slope is bare of soil, leaving the grey stone of
the hills exposed.
name: Base of a Ridge

desc:
The hills are flatter here than those nearby, and have developed a thicker layer
of soil. Grass and wildflowers grow in abundance and thrive on the rich
nutrients. Hundreds of isolated trees dot the hillsides, from a few short and
twisted junipers to tall oaks and yews. A low ridge stands just to the west,
descending as it extends southward.
name: Scattered Trees

desc:
Blackthorns, birches, maples, and a number of other types of trees grow in large
numbers on these hills, yet they tend to be separated from one another and form
only occasional groves, never a forest. Between the trees, grass has taken hold
in most places, giving way only in those areas where bare stone pushes up
through the surface.
name: Lightly-wooded Hills

desc:
The hills come to an abrupt end here, dropping away in tall, vertical cliffs to
the south and east. Saxifrage and mountain avens cling to the rocky sides, but
nothing else has managed to take hold. The hilltop itself is nearly flat and
forms a small plateau coated in moss, grass, and a few small wildflowers. The
northern and western slopes are much gentler and lead into rolling hills.
name: Edge of the Green Hills

desc:
Short, thin-bladed grass covers most of the hillside, interspersed in many
places by large stones and boulders. A scattering of purple and red wildflowers
dot the hillside among the grass. Dozens of trees have managed to find root here
as well, most of them elm or hawthorn, along with a few rowans. A tall, steep
rock wall rises to the west, leading up to a ridgeline far above.
name: Tree-studded Hillside

desc:
This hill is by no means small, but it is dwarfed by the high stony ridge that
runs just to the west. Much of the hillside is covered with boulders and loose
rocks of every size, but in many places grass, shrubs, and even a few trees have
managed to take root. Most of the boulders are rounded with age and coated with
moss, but a few have sharp, broken edges, indicating that rockfalls are still a
possibility here.
name: Hill below a Ridge

desc:
This hill is overshadowed and in the lee of the high hills to the west and the
north. This protected environment has allowed many shrubs, grass, and trees to
thrive. Near the rising slopes, boulders and broken rocks lie where they came to
rest as they rolled or fell from higher ground in ages past.
name: Low Hill in the Shadow of a Ridge

desc:
The hills here can barely be called hills, and the narrow breaks between them
are hardly valleys. This mostly flat landscape is covered in various forms of
vegetation from the grasses and weeds on the hilltops to the bushes and
occasional trees that dot the areas between them.
name: Rolling Hills

desc:
The large, rolling hills in the distance to the north-west have gradually shrunk
into small rolling mounds here that are easy to walk up and down. A multitude of
grasses, shrubs, and weeds have found a place to thrive in the mostly dirt
covered ground. The occasional break where bedrock pokes through the surface
here is evidence of years and years of wind and erosion. To the west, a sheer
rocky bluff rises to the heights far above while to the east a sheer drop off
overlooks a dry gulch that splits the hills.
name: Green Hills

desc:
A shattered hilltop lies strewn about the area. It is not clear what caused the
hill here to be broken into the large boulders and rocky scree that cover the
ground for quite some distance, but the large cliffs to the south-west show
signs of large chunks having been dislodged over the years. The impact of such
large stones would have caused some damage, as would the wind that appears to be
funnelled this way from the towering hillside and any up-drafts that come from
the rocky gulch to the east.
name: Battered Hilltop

desc:
The small mounds that extend from the west and south come to an abrupt halt at
the edge of a gulch that lies below. While the rocky hills that cover the area
have shown that they are more than capable of withstanding the eons of weather
that have played against them, here is proof that they are not impervious and
can eventually be broken down. At the edge of the gulch, the hillock shows signs
of slow deteriation that will eventually claim more of the exposed rocky hilltop
that can be seen here. The exposed and crumbling rocky area here is interspersed
with tufts of grass, standing in stark contrast to the abundent vegetation that
can be seen to the south.
name: Crumbling Hillock at the Edge of a Gulch

desc:
The hills here have no vegetation or soil. They are formed from solid bedrock,
and have been polished smooth by the elements. Only in the small valleys between
the hills where dirt and soil cover the rock can signs of vegetation be found,
struggling to survive the harsh environment.
name: Rocky Hills

desc:
Rough, rocky hills continue to the west, the north, and the south. A sheltered
campsite has been established here; a small spring bubbles up from between the
rocks, and the surrounding grey boulders provide protection from wind and rain.
The campsite is also well hidden from view. To the east there is a sudden break
in the terrain where a gulch has formed. Closer to the edge of the cliff, the
terrain becomes increasingly broken and rocky.
name: Campsite amongst the Broken Hills

desc:
The terrain on these high hills is rough and rocky. Some grass and bushes still
find enough soil to take root. There is a steep drop to the north with
grass-covered hills at its foot. To the west, the hills become increasingly
covered with vegetation, while the rocky terrain continues to the east and the
south.
name: Rocky Hills

desc:
This rocky hilltop stands taller than the surrounding lands. Its northern and
westerns slopes descend abruptly, allowing an unhindered view for anyone wanting
to watch and observe the landscape. Below the steep hillsides, a trail can be
seen as it winds its way through the hilly terrain. Towards the south and east,
the terrain slopes gently down. Colourful flowers dot the thick grass that
covers the nearby hills and valleys.
name: Natural Vantage Point on a Hill Overlooking the Trail

desc:
These hills are bare and barren. The bedrock is exposed and worn smooth by
weather and wind. The little vegetation that grows here is mostly made up of
spindly bushes clustered around rare cracks in the rock, where soil and water
get trapped.
name: Rocky Hills

desc:
The otherwise rolling hills are flatter here, especially to the north, where a
trail cuts through them running east and west. The hills that roll onward in all
other directions are still negotiable with limited effort, with the exception of
several large, thorny bushes that may require a slight detour to the left or
right. The land ascends to the south, eventually entering rockier and more
difficult terrain.
name: Hills Adjoining a Trail

desc:
A series of low, brush-covered hills makes up this part of the Tower Hills. To
the north a winding trail crosses the hills. Even though the hills vary in
height, they are easily traversable by any reasonably healthy traveller. To the
south, a higher hill rises above the landscape.
name: Brush-covered Hills

desc:
These hills are not tall, rising and falling only a little as they roll on in
every direction, yet they still have steep slopes in many spots. There is not
much grass here, but in its place grow wide swaths of brownish-yellow flowers
and a few low shrubs. A number of oaks, willows, and maples grow on the hills as
well, but are not thick enough at any point to be a true forest.
name: Lightly-wooded Hills

desc:
Dozens of small ridges descend from each of the low hilltops here, creating an
array of very narrow valleys between them. Nearly the entire landscape is
covered in various types of grasses and weeds, along with a number of short
bushes. There are some bare patches of stone-laden dirt that stand out in stark
brown contrast to the green hillsides.
name: Rolling Green Hills

desc:
Hundreds of trees once stood on this hilltop, but nearly all of them are now
gone. It appears that a tremendous storm lashed its way through here five or ten
years ago, knocking down all but a few of the trees. Their trunks now lie
rotting on the ground. Many saplings push their way up through the grass, but it
will take years before they reclaim the area.
name: Broken Trees

desc:
Most of this hillside is covered with green weeds and grass, but in several
places small patches of pinkish-purple heather have become dominant. There are
also a number of low privet and witchhazel shrubs growing in scattered masses.
The hillside slopes downward toward the south, becoming almost vertical at its
base.
name: Green Hillside

desc:
This house is not very large, but it is by no means cramped. The residents have
kept their furniture to a minimum, leaving as much free room as possible. The
walls are made from a light golden-brown wood that bears a glassy finish which
helps brighten the room. A small stove sits against the east wall, while a large
rug fills the centre of the room.
name: Small House

desc:
This house is significantly larger than the others in the small village, and is
the only one with more than a single room. The main room contains a couple of
narrow beds along with a small table and several chairs. Shelves line the walls
and bear a large number of carved wooden figurines. A wide doorway stands in the
north wall, leading further into the house.
name: Elven Chief's House

desc:
This kitchen is both spacious and well-apportioned. A dazzling display of pots
and pans hang from hooks along the walls. In addition to a large fireplace,
there is also a stove. A sink and hand-pump stand beneath a broad window that
allows light and fresh air to enter. There is a single long counter extending
for over eight feet against the north wall.
name: Large Kitchen

desc:
This room has been carefully excavated beneath the kitchen. The floor is bare
dirt covered with a thick layer of sand, while the walls are constructed of
small blocks of stone. Wooden racks line most of the walls, some designed for
holding bottles of wine, others for supporting general foodstuffs. There are
also several bins for holding grain and vegetables.
name: Dry Cellar

desc:
This enormous flat hilltop extends in every direction except to the north, where
a steep slope descends to a lower set of hills. The land here is almost bare of
vegetation, leaving the native stone of the hill exposed. Ages of wind and rain
have smoothed the rock, erasing every sharp edge. Other than a few weeds growing
in cracks in the plateau, nothing grows here.
name: Barren Plateau

desc:
This flat hilltop extends to the south and east, but drops off sharply in the
other directions. Short, coarse grass is interspersed with patches of bare grey
stone. A few stunted cedars have managed to take hold here, their trunks bent
away from the prevaling western wind that frequently pushes its way across this
plateau.
name: Barren Plateau

desc:
Very little is able to grow on this exposed hilltop. Despite the flat surface,
no soil has accumulated here, apparently being scoured away by rain and wind
before any plants have time to take root. In most places the bare stone of the
hill is visible, but in some places it is coated with a thin layer of pale blue
lichen. A few small patches of bright green moss adorn the stone as well.
name: Barren Plateau

desc:
The plateau is not entirely flat here, sloping downward as it extends an arm
north into the eastern edge of the Tower Hills. However, the edges of the
plateau fall away in steep cliffs long before the slope can reach the ground
below. It would be impossible to safely descend any of the cliffs, leaving the
south as the only direction to leave this place.
name: Rocky Plateau

desc:
This ridge in the Tower Hills rises as it extends to the north, while dropping
off to the south. Very little vegetation manages to cling to the bare rocks,
with only a few tufts of grass and weeds springing up from cracks in the stone.
The sides of the ridge are very steep and smooth, their grey stone providing no
footholds to allow a safe descent.
name: Sloping Ridge

desc:
The flat portion of the ridgeline is very wide here, easily spanning a hundred
feet or more. While it does tilt slightly downward toward the east, the ridge is
level enough to allow travel without unusual difficulty. Very little grows on
the rocky surface of the ridge beyond a few tufts of grass, yet somehow several
small pine trees have managed to take root and thrive here.
name: Broad Ridge

desc:
A narrow ridgeline ends abruptly here as it butts up against a higher ridge to
the north and west. The ridge forms a wide, nearly flat shelf, rising only
gradually to the south. The ground is littered with stones that have split off
from the higher rock walls, ranging in size from pebbles to enormous boulders.
Sparse grass and a few low shrubs are the only vegetation.
name: Low Ridge

desc:
The ridge here is wide and mostly flat on top, though it does fall away quickly
to either side. The ridgeline is bare of any soil or vegetation, exposing the
medium grey stone that forms the hill. The ridge narrows as it continues to the
north, while as it moves south it bends to the east and begins to descend.
name: Broad Ridge

desc:
This thin ridgeline runs almost directly north and south, remaining level for
most of the way. There is little room to manoeuvre from side to side, but no
difficulty in moving along the ridge. Steep, barren hillsides of grey stone fall
away to either side, both of them being too sheer and smooth to allow any chance
of travel in those directions.
name: Narrow Ridge

desc:
As ridges from the east, west, and south come together, they form a small
plateau. The ground here is mostly flat, with nothing but the occasional boulder
or clumps of tough weeds braving the exposed terrain. Far to the east, the hills
gradually disappears to give way to green fields and forests.
