name: High Ridge

desc:
This cave extends back only twenty feet or so from its mouth, yet that appears
to be more than sufficient for its inhabitant. There are very few furnishings to
be seen. A low wooden table sits against one wall, while a woven straw mat lies
on the floor by the other. A small scorched area at the opening of the cave
attests to frequent fires built there. Clean, dry sand coats the floor.
name: Dry Cave

desc:
A gently sloping ridge descends from rugged highlands to the east, linking them
with a flat hilltop to the north. The ridge is covered with grass and small
bushes; some large boulders on the western slope are overgrown with moss.
name: Sloping Ridge

desc:
A ridgeline descends from the west and bends sharply to the north where it meets
a wide hilltop. While this area is traversable, the ridge is narrow and the
terrain rough. The sides of the ridge fall away in steep descents covered in
loose rocks and scree.
name: Bending Ridgeline

desc:
A massive hilltop sits here with ridgelines extending, like spokes on a wheel,
to the west and south. To the north, east, and west the hills and ridges
continue on a mostly flat trajectory. To the south, a climbing ridgeline ascends
higher into the Tower Hills. A few small bushes and tufts of grass have found
cracks to take root in, otherwise the hill is mostly bare rock.
name: Hilltop

desc:
Bluffs to the north, the east, and the south marks the end of a wide hilltop.
The land below is dotted with small rolling hills. To the southwest a peak
towers over the surrounding landscape, and it appears to be the highest point in
this region of the Tower Hills.
name: Atop Towering Bluffs

desc:
A narrow shelf connects the areas to the east and west of here as it runs along
the north side of a high cliff face. The rock wall to the south is a near
vertical drop with no signs of anything to hold on to. No vegetation grows on
the shelf aside from a couple of struggling, miserable weeds.
name: Narrow Shelf

desc:
This large plateau appears to have once been a large hill, but time has eroded
away the top. It also serves as a crossroad of sorts for the lines of hills that
run in all directions. The ground slopes gradually down to lowlands to the north
and west, while lines of hills rise to the south and east.
name: Fractured Hilltop

desc:
A narrow ridge rises to form a promontory overlooking the rolling hills below.
The hillside is uneven and shows signs of cracking near the edge, and fallen
rocks litter the ground below. Vegetation is sparse, but some grass and brush
help hold the soil together and thus slow the deterioration at the edge of the
cliffs.
name: Ridge Overlooking the Tower Hills

desc:
The hills that continue onward to the north and east come to a halt against the
towering cliffs that lie to the west and south. The cliffs provide shelter from
most of the elements, allowing brush, grasses, and the occasional stunted tree
to flourish here. Boulders and rocky debris cover the vegetation in some areas
where it has fallen from the heights above. There is a looming tunnel entrance
to the south.
name: Sheltered Hillock

desc:
Tall hills rise against the southern skyline, while the smaller, rolling hills
to the north and east are lower. While the hills are possible to traverse,
caution may be required as loose rocks and scree cover some slopes. The
occasional patch of grass, shrub, and even a few lonely trees dot the
surrounding landscape.
name: Rolling Hills

desc:
The smaller hills in this region of the Tower Hills lie in the shadow of a large
peak to the south. The steep southern cliffs are rugged and cracked, and the
ground beneath them is covered in fallen rocks. Elsewhere the hills are covered
with grass, brush, and weeds.
name: Hill in the Shadow of a Peak

desc:
The vertical walls surrounding this deadend to the east, south and west leave no
means to move in those directions. An expanse of rolling hills stretch outwards
to the north, with no end in sight. The ground here is littered with scree and
boulders, possibly from landslides though there are moss and weeds growing on
them.
name: Rocky Deadend

desc:
The rocky hills to the north form a barrier from the harsh northern winds that
would otherwise sweep across this area. Grass and bushes seem to find the
shelter agreeable, and a wide flora of plants grow here. To the west and south,
the hills suddenly terminate as they run up against steep, towering cliffs. The
hills continue to the east, undulating gently. there are some animal paths
leading in and out of this area, and a small spring bubbles up in the middle of
this protected part of the Tower Hills, making it an ideal resting spot.
name: Sheltered Hills

desc:
Hundreds of trees stand on these hills, making it difficult to see too far in
any single direction. None of them are particularly tall, with only a single
tall oak rising above thirty feet. The trees are mostly isolated from one
another, with only a couple of denser clumps. Low grass surrounds them, except
where creases of bare stone break their way through the soil.
name: Lightly-wooded Hills

desc:
These gently rolling hills are covered in a thin layer of grass, interspersed
with chickweed, sowthistles, stinging nettles, and other varieties of weeds. A
few low bushes and trees also dot the landscape, most growing in isolation from
one another. The greenery is broken by sheer walls of rock in many places, none
of them very wide or rising more than a dozen feet or so.
name: Green Hills

desc:
Scattered trees dot almost every small hill that can be seen from here. The
trees appear to get thicker to the south where they form occasional copses. Some
areas show scattered weeds and scrub brush, while others show the dirt having
been completely blown away down to the bedrock underneath.
name: Tree-covered Hills

desc:
Several of the hills in this region of the Tower Hills bear scars in the form of
cracks and fissures. While mostly shallow, care must still be taken when
travelling across the broken landscape. Weeds, grass and small bushes seem to
find the conditions here agreeable.
name: Broken Hills

desc:
Centuries of erosion have reduced this region to what would more adequately be
called an elevated field. Wide, grass-covered mounds are all that remains of
what were once towering hills, at least if the surrounding landscape is an
indication of what this area once looked like. The hills become increasingly
bare towards the west. The exposed bedrock in that direction shows signs of
cracks and fissures.
name: Worn Hills

desc:
Rows of low, rugged hills make up this area of the Tower Hills. Most of them are
covered in sparse vegetation, green and yellow wildgrass and some small bushes.
In contrast, the meadow to the south at the foot of the hills, is vividly green
with a multitude of colourful flowers.
name: Hill Overlooking a Meadow

desc:
The hills overlook a small rocky footpath to the east and a wider, but badly
overgrown trail in the south. The ground is covered with rocks of varying shapes
and sizes. Small green and brown creeping vines climb up the larger boulders, as
if a natural net had been cast upon the surface of the stone.
name: Tower Hills

desc:
Thick branches spread out in every direction from the trunk, many wide enough to
walk on without danger. Thousands of thinner branches and twigs create a thick
lacework of wood and leaves, blocking out any view of the land beyond. A few
tattered fragments of snakeskin hang from some of the branches.
name: Tall Oak

desc:
These hills, low with gentle slopes, are covered in scrub brush and grass. The
hills expand to the east and south while a deep gulch scars the land to the
west. Towards the south the land become increasingly rocky, and features
fissures, cracks, and steep cliffs. A rising ridge to the north follows the
eastern side of the gulch.
name: Rolling Hills

desc:
A narrow strip of land runs along a sheer drop to the north and west. Down
below, a dry gulch can be seen running to the north and south with a small side
passage directly to the north. As the ridge continues to the east from here, it
climbs upward gently. A thin layer of top soil covers the ground allowing grass
and small bushes to grow.
name: Towering Ridgeline

desc:
This meandering ridgeline is covered with loose rocks and small bushes. It makes
a wide north-to-west bend around a deep gulch. The bottom of the gulch is a
distant grey-yellow smear of stones and boulders atleast a hundred feet below
the ridge.
name: Bending Ridgeline

desc:
In the absence of trees, these hills are dominated by dense, green bushes. From
atop the tallest hill the view stretches for miles across hills to the south and
east. An impenetrable wall of dense bushes, dozens of yards across, separates
the hills from a steep ridge to the north.
name: Bush-covered Hilltop

desc:
Hills rise and fall, some small and others large. Some have steep sides covered
in scree and rocks, while others are overgrown with grass and bushes. A tower
stands on a hill to the north-east, a white pillar rising a hundred feet or more
above the Tower Hills.
name: Tower Hills

desc:
The ground here is mostly devoid of greenery, though a few valiant shrubs and
bushes endure the almost desolate landscape. To the north, a prominent hill with
very steep sides rises up blocking travel in that direction. The ground slopes
somewhat to the east, leading down to a small dirt path.
name: Tower Hills

desc:
This rolling meadow rises and falls in low, uneven folds. Two species of grass
grow here. One is shorter, with broad, dark blades. The other is taller,
thinner, and paler. Flowers in every imaginable colour are scattered across the
meadow, in many places crowding out the grass entirely. Tall hills rise to both
the north and west, while south of here the ground remains level but grows
rockier.
name: Flowery Meadow

desc:
This corridor is constructed of large square blocks of grey stone. The corridor
leads into a room to the north and continues to the east, where the end is
hidden in darkness. Dust lies in a thick layer on the floor. There are
footprints in the dust which have clearly been disturbed several times recently.
There are iron torch holders set in the wall at even intervals, now covered in
flaking red-brown rust.
name: Stone-walled Corridor

desc:
This room is well constructed. The walls are of square, grey stones and fixed in
place with mortar. The floor is very even and made of large, flat slabs of the
same grey stone as the walls. The ceiling is arched and there is not a crack to
be seen even though the room does not seem to have been in use for a long time.
Numerous dusty shelves of sturdy wood line the walls and two large cabinets gape
empty.
name: Empty Larder

desc:
Bigger than any cave that an animal could have dug out, this den must be
natural. Hard, dark earth composes the walls, floor, and ceiling of this
dwelling. Numerous roots hang down from the roof near the back of the cave, some
so long in length they reach the floor several feet below. Tufts of white,
silvery fur lie scattered upon the ground.
name: Wolf Den

desc:
Fractured pieces of stone have come to a rest on the barren ground. Small
lizards and other tiny creatures scurry about the broken rocks. To sustain the
little life that is found here a water source must be nearby. The canyon walls
continue to rise up to the north and south leaving only the east-west path along
the floor of the canyon.
name: Canyon Floor

desc:
Several loose rocks have worked free from a large cleft in the wall of the
canyon and now lie scattered about the foot of the fissure. No vegetation can
grow in the desolate bedrock that makes up the canyon floor. Shale rocks lining
the edge of the crack provide handholds that would allow a climber to reach the
wider ledges further up and, eventually, provide a way out of the canyon.
name: Canyon Floor

desc:
The expansive shale canyon comes to an end. Multiple varieties of weeds spring
up around a small pool. Small animals scatter under the rocks away from the
water when the slightest of disturbances occur in the surroundings. The only way
out is to the east with the canyon walls extending to the north, west and south.
name: Canyon Floor

desc:
A grey, moss-covered rockface rises high above the surrounding hills. Large
rocks and boulders lie scattered across the slope at its foot, half-buried in
the ground as if fallen from a great height. Tall grass, green and wild,
flourishes on the slopes of nearby hills and in the valleys while the hilltops
themselves are mostly bare and barren of vegetation.
name: Tower Hills by a Ridge

desc:
The hills rise to their rocky, barren tops then dip sharply to dry valleys where
naught but hardy shrubs and wild tufts of coarse grass grow. Large rocks and
boulders lie scattered across the hills to the north, where a wide, steep ridge
blocks further travel. A trail can be seen further to the south and east, making
its way across the Tower Hills.
name: Tower Hills by a Ridge

desc:
A tall ridge rises in the north above the Tower Hills. It is steep and covered
with loose rock and dirt; unsuitable for climbing. At the ridge's base, boulders
of all sizes lie scattered, broken and half-buried in the hard ground. The
surrounding hills are sparse with vegetation as only hardy bushes and
wild-looking grass cling to the stony ground.
name: Tower Hills by a Ridge

desc:
There are many signs of game trails among the low grass and sparse shrubbery
growing here. In between the patches of vegetation where the earth is exposed,
hoof-marks are visible, likely from deer or goat. From atop a tall hill, the
view reveals an unremarkable expanse of more hills in all directions. However, a
thin, brown scar marking the route of a wagon-trail can be seen to be curving
from the east to the south.
name: Undulating Grass Hills

desc:
Stretching for miles toward the east and south, the tall but gentle hills seem
to ramble on without end. The land in this part of the Tower Hills is lush with
thick grass, moving gracefully with the slightest breeze. Here and there, slim
trees stand tall. In the distance to the southeast, a trail can be seen snaking
through the rolling hills. To the north and west, the hills end abruptly,
revealing wide plains below.
name: Grass-covered Hills

desc:
Undulating in long gentle waves, the hills stretch out in all directions.
Carpeted with thick, green grass down to the valley floors, the land sports
thriving bushes and the occasional slim tree. The landscape is wild although
there are bare spots where grass has been grazed and trampled, probably by deer
or sheep. To the south and east, a trail meanders lazily through valley floors
among the hills.
name: Grass-covered Hills

desc:
Grassy hills rise and fall in gentle green waves, making for easy walking even
though there are no trails to follow. The vegetation, mostly lush grass
interspersed with various bushes and shrubs, stands several feet tall in the
valleys, while it thins out considerably on the nearby slopes and hilltops.
name: Grass-covered Hills

desc:
These grass-covered hills should be ideal for herbivores and indeed there are
many signs of deer; tracks are plentiful and obvious and droppings litter the
ground. The land rises and falls through brush-filled valleys punctuated by
sparsely vegetated hillocks. Towards the southwest a green valley lies embedded
in the hills.
name: Grass-covered Hills

desc:
The hills here overlook a valley to the west. The hills continue to rise and
fall to the north, the east, and the south. Throughout the vegetation that
covers them, mainly various grasses and bushes, trails from various animals
hints at a rich wildlife.
name: Hill Overlooking a Valley

desc:
Wild, rolling hills expand in all directions. A man-made trail follows the
valley floors in-between hills as it makes its way through the Tower Hills to
the south-east. The hills are covered with thick grasses, and animal trails are
in clear evidence throughout the area. Thicker vegetation flourish between the
hills where the wind can not affect them as much.
name: Rolling Hills

desc:
At one point this hill was covered in heather and other hardy shrubs. Years of
exposure to the biting wind and a lack of rain have taken its toll leaving only
the dead, woody remains behind. There is little sign of animal life here, even
the insects shun these barren hills.
name: Barren Hills

desc:
This prominent peak towers above the surrounding countryside, providing a
commanding view of the wagon trail that sweeps gracefully around the base of the
hill to the north and west. There is little shelter on this exposed spot, and
the soil has been washed away into the valleys below, leaving little chance for
any vegetation to grow here.
name: Rocky Peak

desc:
Standing in the shadow of the higher peak to the north, this bare and infertile
hill supports little in the way of bushes or trees. A few exposed patches of
rocks, worn smooth with the action of rain and frost over hundreds of years, are
all that mark the summit.
name: Barren Hills

desc:
A landscape of gently sloping, grass-covered hills expands to the east and
north. In the shallow valleys between hills, brush and even the occasional tree
makes an appearance. Steep cliffs rise to the south and the west. The bluffs at
the top of the cliffs no doubt offer a spectacular view of the Tower Hills.
name: Rolling Hills

desc:
A deep gulch to the east and south forms an impassable gap between these hills
and other, similar hills beyond. The vegetation is limited to spots of grass
that are scattered across the shattered rocks that line the hilltops. Once, long
ago, water may have run through here to help form the gulch. However, there are
no signs of water anywhere in the area now.
name: Edge above a Gulch

desc:
The terrain here forms a depression between hills to the east and to the west.
There is a slope down into a small valley to the north, and the land appears to
be somewhat flat to the south. It is quite easy to navigate north and south
through this dip, and it is also possible to travel up the slopes to the east
and the west.
name: Between Towering Hills

desc:
A rare break in the hills can be found here. The valley runs downward to the
west and appears from the south. At one point, a river may have run through here
and cut its way through the hills to cause this valley. However, no water can be
seen here any more. The river may have expended itself in cutting through these
age old cliffs.
name: In a Valley

desc:
This hill is sparsely vegetated by grass and shrubs. A valley can be seen to the
north, but there does not appear to be a way down to it from here. A small hill
can be seen to the west that could be easily ascended. To the south and east,
sloping hills can be seen continuing toward the horizon.
name: Hill Overlooking a Valley

desc:
This hill is slightly higher than the surrounding hills, and a small area can be
surveyed from here. To the west and south, smaller hills continue for miles.
Some broken large rocks lie here with moss and ferns growing in their shadows.
name: Top of a Small Hill

desc:
To the east, the hillside ends at the edge of an abrupt cliff face. A valley can
be seen below, but there are no handholds to allow one to climb down here. In
other directions, the sloping hillsides can easily be traversed with the highest
area being to the south.
name: Edge of a Cliff

desc:
Long, shallow ravines cross the hills, giving them a broken appearance. Grass
and flowers cover the hillsides and show that, although the area is rocky,
vegetation can manage to find places to grow. A trail winds its way through the
hills to the south and east.
name: Broken Hills

desc:
Gently rolling hills extend in all directions. Several types of grass grow in
the less rocky areas between hills. There are scattered signs of small game
throughout the area such as droppings and foraged vegetation. The slopes of the
hills are not steep enough to pose a problem for anyone travelling through here.
name: Rolling Hills

desc:
Rolling hills dominate the landscape for miles in all directions. The only minor
break in their continuity is to the south and east where a trail follows the
flatter terrain. Signs of small animals can be seen amongst rocks and tufts of
vegetation.
name: Break in the Hills

desc:
Bracken and rough grass cover patches of the otherwise bleak and barren
hillside. Small strands of greyish wool hang from the bracken where sheep have
tried to graze and snagged their coats. The ground slopes gently away in every
direction save for the south where a deep ravine splits this hill away from the
ridgeline beyond.
name: Barren Hills

desc:
From the top of a hill, a trail is visible to the west and north. The hill ends
abruptly and no way of climbing down from here can be seen. To the east, the
hill's eastern slope descends gently. The rocky, scraggly brush-covered hills
continue to roll onward to the south and can be seen to the west across the
trail as well.
name: Atop a Hill

desc:
A ravine can be seen cutting through the land below to the east and north. The
hills come together and form a minor upward slope to the west. Southward, the
hills continue for miles and miles. Grasses dominate the landscape with the
occasional shrub making an appearance along with a few solitary trees.
name: Atop a Promontory

desc:
The sloping hills that continue to the south and west appear to come to an end
somewhere to the north. They come to a halt immediately to the east where a
cliff overlooks a ravine. A few smaller bushes and wildflowers disturb the sea
of grass that covers the land.
name: Barren Hills

desc:
These hills harbour a variety of weeds, mosses, fungi and other small plants.
Yet for all the plantlife that can be found here, the landscape looks almost
barren; it is dusty and clad in shades of grey and brown. A ravine cuts through
the hills to the north, at the bottom of a steep slope.
name: Above a Ravine

desc:
The hills slope downward to the north, and a ravine cuts through the hills in
that direction. The ground is rocky with some small plants and shrubs lending
some colour to the otherwise grey-brown hillsides. To the west, a sheer rock
face makes travel in that direction impossible. A small rise can be seen to the
east; it appears to lead out of the cut ravine channel.
name: Slope Descending into a Ravine

desc:
The bottom of this ravine is dry and level. The ground is covered with loose
gravel and struggling weeds. A slope ascends into the hills that abut the ravine
to the south. In other directions, the ravine floor continues but it is not
clear where it goes, or what is to be found in those branches.
name: Ravine Floor

desc:
The ravine is deep and narrow with imposing cliff-walls and a gravel bed
underfoot. Clearly the area was once host to a river that carved its way through
the rock but has long since dried up. To the east, the fragile cliff-walls have
crumbled sending piles of shattered rock into the ravine, blocking any chance of
further travel.
name: Rockfall in a Ravine

desc:
A ravine ends here with the only possible exits being to the east and north. To
the east, the floor of the ravine continues while to the north, a rocky scrabble
can be seen that looks navigable. However, coming back that way may be difficult
due to the loose scree and crushed gravel.
name: End of a Ravine

desc:
A gorge cuts through steep-sided cliffs, gradually narrowing until it reaches a
dead end to the east. Rising high above, the steep canyon walls enclose a flat,
oval area, littered with fragments of rocks that have fallen from the cliffs
above. Upon the ground, small tracks in the sand disappear under some rocks.
name: Narrowing Ravine

desc:
A majestic hill dominates the landscape, rising above the surrounding, lower
hills. To the north, a channel runs downward from the hill into a ravine. A
small ridge slopes slightly downward to the west and south. Scattered moss and
lichen cover the broken scree that litters the ground.
name: Atop a Hill

desc:
To the south, a copse of trees can be seen at the bottom of a steep, smooth
cliff that shows no signs of being traversable. A similar cliff forms a natural
border to the east where the land becomes more flat and fertile, and a forest
borders the hills. The cliff edge that forms this perch continues westward from
here, whereas the terrain to the north consists of sparsely vegetated, rolling
hills.
name: Rocky Perch

desc:
Rocky, scree-covered slopes descend from this rocky peak in all directions but
to the east. A ridge-like series of cliffs extends towards the east. They are
narrow and broken but look stable enough for the moment. Of the three slopes,
only the northern one is traversable, being slightly less steep and rocky than
the southern and the western ones.
name: Rocky Peak

desc:
This hilly area is covered in broken stone and large boulders. A slightly taller
hill can be seen to the east, while a ridge runs to the west. The rolling
terrain continues to the north and south. Tufts of grass and limited vegetation
struggle to scour the ground for nourishment.
name: Barren Hills

desc:
A ridgeline follows a deep gulch that can be seen to the west of here. The
ridgeline continue to the south, and then bends sharply to the east where it
meets up with rolling hills. Is is narrow and rocky, but travel along it is
doable as long as one does not venture too far to the east or west.
name: Meandering Ridgeline

desc:
A narrow, meandering ridgeline runs to the north and south. There is loose scree
to the west, where a deep gully can be seen at the foot of the scree-covered
slope. No vegetation grows here as the surroundings are covered in broken rock,
and with the winds constantly buffeting the area germination is almost
impossible.
name: Meandering Ridgeline

desc:
A sloping narrow ridgeline runs to the north and east here. No path exists down
the steep south-western hillside that descends into a gulch. Broken scree that
makes up every colour of grey and black litters the ridgeline, a sign of ages of
weather buffeting the hills.
name: Meandering Ridgeline

desc:
A narrow, easily traversible ridgeline runs from the west to the south here,
descending as it goes. A deep gorge borders the ridge to the south-west, while
hilly terrain extends for miles to the north and north-east. To the east,
another sheer drop ends at a small circular clearing surrounded by hills.
name: Meandering Ridgeline

desc:
A meandering ridgeline extending to the north and south intersects with a second
ridgeline which is narrower, but straight and comfortably traversable. A hill
lies to the east, a mile or two along the narrow ridgeline. A long drop on the
western side of the meandering ridgeline ends at the bottom of a gulch.
name: Meandering Ridgeline

desc:
A timeworn path winds its way through the hills, ever climbing, until it reaches
the top of the ridgeline far to the east. The path is surrounded by many patches
of weeds and grass. The view here is exceptional; an uninterrupted panorama of a
dense forest to the southeast, extending several miles until a magnificent,
white tower dominates the skyline.
name: Rising Goat Trail

desc:
The ground slopes sharply upwards as the natural trail reaches the top of the
hill. Bushes grow on both sides of the trail, their lower leaves gnawed upon by
small folivores. Beyond the bushes on the southern part of the trail, a dense
forest can be seen and, beyond that, an imposing white tower.
name: Goat Trail

desc:
Walls of steep rock surround this narrow, shallow ravine. The majority of the
rocky walls have been smoothed out from exposure to water and wind through the
ages, although in places small landslides have thrown fresh, jagged scree onto
the floor of the ravine. To the west, the ravine opens up and climbs out onto an
open hillside while, to the north, the canyon narrows considerably.
name: Bend in a Ravine

desc:
A narrow ravine tapers off to a dead end here. The sides are steep and smooth
while the bottom of the ravine is covered with loose rocks and the footing is
treacherous. The ravine widens slightly to the south, which seems to be the only
way to move from here. Several thorny bushes grow among the rocks and boulders
along the ravine's floor; they provide excellent hiding places for reptiles,
snakes and other small animals, should any make this ravine their hunting
ground.
name: Bottom of a Ravine

desc:
A trodden trail takes a sharp turn to the east and south here. The vegetation
surrounding the trail is sparse and mainly consists of small patches of weeds
and grass, joined by the odd tree. Even though the trail seem to provide the
only way to the top of the hill, it seems to be almost completely unused.
name: Bend in a Goat Trail

desc:
On these hilltops, grass and bushes grow rampantly. A narrow trail winds its way
through the hills, rarely taking the easier path and often straying very close
to the edge of the steep cliffs to the north. The floor of a canyon is visible
at the foot of the cliffs. To the south, a flat, verdant forest can be seen
flanked by rising hills to the east and west.
name: Hazardous Goat Trail

desc:
A goat trail follows a ridge of hills towards the north and east. A steep
decline to the south ends at a vast expanse of plains. Hoof marks on the ground
leads north and east along the trail. On the northern side of the trail a small
circular structure that looks like a well is built out of rocks. Patches of moss
grow on the stones, and small weeds sprout from the crevices in-between the
rocks. The water in the well is dried out, and there are no other ruins in sight
to explain why a well was built here.
name: Meandering Goat Trail

desc:
The landscape here is composed mostly of short, stunted patches of grass and the
occasional bush. Hoof marks mar the path that runs north and west heavily,
churning the ground into a tumultuous mix of rocks, sand, and trodden grass. To
the south, the ground becomes treacherously steep and rocky, before flattening
out into an expansive grassland that stretches far into the distance.
name: Narrow Goat Trail

desc:
The ground here is narrow and uneven. Grass, bushes, and various stones cover
the hilly terrain. Towards the north, a serrated escarpment tumbles downward
into a wide canyon, preventing immediate travel in that direction. To the south,
the path descends at uneven intervals, twisting its way around large rocks. Upon
the ground are small tracks, barely discernible against the hard hearth, leading
to and from a small hole in the side of a small mound.
name: Narrow Goat Trail

desc:
An artesian spring bubbles to the surface between two large sheets of hard, grey
rock. The ground is significantly softer than the surrounding area, having been
permeated by water for quite some time. A thick, vibrant bed of grass flanks the
trail on all sides. In several places the flora has been chewed down to the
ground, where hoof prints can be seen deeply embedded in the soft, pliable soil.
name: Twisting Goat Trail

desc:
The rocky hollow is barren apart from some evergreen shrubs growing in the rock
crevices. The terrain here is rough and littered with angled rocks and boulders
making it quite difficult to traverse. Meandering among the rocky hills, a
beaten path winds its way southward under the shade of the cliffs.
name: End of a Goat Trail

desc:
This is a roomy cave. The floor is covered in wet sand, and there is a steady
but slow drip of water down the walls. Fungus and moss grows all over the walls,
and the air smells like damp earth, with a slight hint of decay. A small amount
of light comes in from the hole in the northern rocky surface.
name: Damp Cave

desc:
The long, gentle slopes of this lone hilltop continue for miles in all
directions. The hill is covered with grass and low vegetation. A few large
boulders and rock formations, covered with moss and lichen, dot the slopes. The
slope to the north descends into a wooded hollow that looks like a dead-end.
name: Lone Hill

desc:
A small, wooded hollow is nestled among the Tower Hills here. The only feasible
way in or out of the hollow is a slope to the south. In every other direction,
the steep, rocky hillsides conspire to create this wooded dead-end. The area is
lush with vegetation as many trees cover the valley floor.
name: Wooded Hollow

desc:
The ground slopes heavily downwards from the west, continuing its rapid descent
eastward until the landscape abruptly gives way to a vertical escarpment below.
Gazing further east, the tops of a dense, coniferous forest can be seen tucked
between rising hills on all sides except to the north, where the green forest
grows ever-onward.
name: Sloping Tower Hill

desc:
The hills here form many unique rock formations. Northward, the hills give way
to a long slope leading to a lone, majestic hilltop. Some faint signs of some
ancient structures can be seen, but their meaning and shape have been lost for
eons. Small bushes and shrubs cover the low lands in-between slopes, while
grasses cover the slopes and peaks of the hills.
name: Draw in the Hills

desc:
The prominent crown of this hill flattens out into a wide grassy plateau, upon
which a circle of stone pillars has been raised. Their purpose is unknown, lost
in the mists of time. It is possible that this spot was chosen because of the
commanding view of the surrounding countryside. Low bushes and shrubs cover the
ground, varying in size and colour.
name: Standing Stones upon a Tower Hill

desc:
Barren hills to the north and west meet here and their slopes form a descent to
the south, into a shadowy gulch. There is little vegetation as the ground is
covered by a lot of rocky scree. The few bushes and shrubs that do find these
conditions agreeable are thorny, tough-looking plants.
name: Sloping Ravine

desc:
A gulch begins at the foot of a rugged, scree-covered slope to the north. The
dry gulch is nestled between a ridgeline to the east and hills to the west. It
runs north and south, with sheer rocky walls that prohibit climbing out to the
east and west. Vegetation is sparse with naught but small, thorny shrubs and
weeds that struggly to survive on the dry, shaded canyon floor.
name: Dry Gulch

desc:
A small off-shoot of the otherwise straight north-south running gulch lies to
the west. While it appears as though water once ran through here to assist in
the creation of this gulch, none is in evidence anymore. Small boulders and
rocky scrabble litters the ground everywhere which could provide places for
animals to hide. Most of the bottom of the gulch is cast in shadows as even the
noon sun can never quite reach it.
name: Dry Gulch

desc:
A north-south running gulch cuts through the hills here. Rocky scrabble covers
the ground, providing shade most of the day. There is little vegetation growing
here and no water. Small cracks along the west rocky wall might provide
excellent handholds for a climber who want to reach the rocky ledge higher on
the gulch's steep side.
name: Dry Gulch

desc:
A narrow gulch in the surrounding hills continues north and south. Very little
vegetation grows in it, and the majority of the ground is covered by rocky
scree. In this area, the rock walls that block travel to the east and west, show
little signs of wear, and attempting to climb them seem futile.
name: Dry Gulch

desc:
A narrow, dry gulch ends here at the rockface that blocks its southern end.
Similar rockwalls to the east and west form a natural dead-end cast in perpetual
gloom. The only ways out of this gulch is either to follow it north, or attempt
to climb the least steep of the sides, the western one.
name: Rocky Deadend

desc:
A small offshoot of the gulch which runs to the west can be found here,
surrounded by cliffs that define the walls of the area. No hand holds can be
seen along the walls and escaping this dead-end by climbing is impossible. A few
boulders lie on the ground, with a few small shrubs and weeds growing scattered
about.
name: Rocky Deadend

desc:
This north-facing cavern is shaded from direct sunlight by a stone shelf that
overhangs the narrow entrance to the north. The view is highly restricted by
cliffs that flank the ravine that lies outside this gloomy hollow. There is
naught but rocks to be seen in this desolate grotto.
name: Below an Overhang

desc:
A rocky shelf clings to the rock wall, halfway between the hills above and the
gulch floor below. There appears to be a way to descend to the bottom of the
gulch by using some small cracks as handholds, but the wall above is sheer and
smooth.
name: On a Rocky Shelf

desc:
Layers of folded rock are exposed, clearly showing the effect of the violent
upheavals that must have occurred to create these hills. A stream of water
gushes forth from one break in the rocks, creating a small pool before draining
downhill and vanishing again beneath the ground. Deep ruts beside the pool hint
that someone often comes here with a wagon. The ruts run to the west, but they
vanish on the bare rock and their destination is impossible to determine.
name: Spring in the Broken Hills

desc:
A broad cavern has been eroded into the stone of the ridge, sloping quickly
downward as it extends to the north. Trickles of water run down the walls of the
cave, leaving the uneven rocky floor slick and treacherous. About thirty feet
from the entrance, the floor of the cavern vanishes below the surface of a dark
pool of water.
name: Watery Cave

desc:
This small earthen cave is little more than a hollow in the ground, yet it is
more than large enough for several people. There are signs that the cave has
been deliberately expanded, though not by human hands. Only a few roots poke
through the ceiling; all of the others have been cut off level with the soil.
Tiny tunnels snake off into the walls, too small for even a hobbit to follow.
name: Beneath a Rotten Trunk

desc:
The residents of this house keep it spotless. Every piece of furniture matches
with the rest and sits in a carefully arranged location. There is no dust or
dirt to be seen on the floor, or on any other surface. Colourful drapes adorn
the windows, drawn back by ties of the same material to allow fresh air to flow
through the house.
name: Tidy House

desc:
Some great upheaval in the distant past has twisted and folded the landscape,
creating swells and dips reminiscent of the sea yet frozen forever in stone. The
ground is cracked in many places and littered with thousands of fragments of
rock, some dulled with age, others much fresher and still sharp-edged. Most of
the stone is medium grey, but there are patches of orange, brown, and even
black.
name: Tower Hills

desc:
This hill does not rise as high as most of the Tower Hills, and is dwarfed in
comparison to a much higher hilltop just to the north. Little soil has managed
to accumulate on the hill's surface, leaving most of it as exposed rock and
preventing much of any vegetation from growing here. Other than a few short
flowers, only two small, twisted pines have managed to take root.
name: Low Hillside

desc:
The trail bends and twists so much that it is difficult to maintain a straight
course for more than a dozen yards at a time. The path dodges around boulders
and upthrusts of bare stone, yet manages to remain nearly level despite the
hills through which it passes. Pale yellow and pink blossoms decorate the tops
of tall green stems, while purple glasswort hugs the rocky ground.
name: Winding Faded Trail

desc:
This narrow ridgeline rises over a deep, lush valley to the east. The centre of
the ridge is grey stone, bare and lifeless, but on both sides soil and grass
cling to the slopes, giving it almost the appearance of a roadway cut through a
meadow. A deep crack splits the ground to the west between the descending side
of the ridge and a tall plateau that rises just beyond.
name: Grass-covered Ridge

desc:
The trail looks like it has not received much use recently and is beginning to
fade. It runs roughly north and south but winds somewhat in order to remain
fairly flat. A tall plateau dominates the landscape to the east, preventing any
sunlight from reaching the trail before noon. As a result, there is not much in
the way of vegetation here, only a few stray weeds scattered among the rocks.
name: Faded Trail

desc:
Bending from the south to the east, a trail follows the flow of the hills,
avoiding steep slopes and hilltops. This trail is obviously man-made and shows
signs of being used to haul wagons through the area. However, signs of the wild
reclaiming the land appear occasionally, as though the path has been used less
in recent times. An old way station butts up against the steep hillside to the
northwest.
name: Wagon Trail by a Way Station

desc:
This stable has a broad corridor between stalls on either side. Each stall has a
hefty amount of hay, and a trough containing fresh water. The walls of the
stable are old, but clean and in good condition. They are made of broad wooden
planks, while the floor has been paved with large flat stones. There are sturdy
double doors leading out of the stable to the east, and another exit to the
north, leading further inside the way station.
name: Way Station's Stable

desc:
This a small storage room. There are shelves, crates, cabinets, and barrels all
over the room, and seemingly all surfaces have been filled with boxes, sacks and
various other miscellaneous items. There are two exits, towards the south and
east, and a tiny window giving a view to the north.
name: Storage Room

desc:
This is the spacious main room of a Way Station in the Tower Hills. A broad
counter stands in the centre of the cabin; this is where the travellers take
care of their business with the station personel. Next to it stands a roaring
fireplace, and in the area infront of the fireplace there are some tables and
chairs. A row of bunk beds stands against the back wall, capable of hosting a
dozen tired travellers.
name: Way Station of Tower Hills

desc:
This chimney looks as if it goes straight up on all sides for at least a couple
of dozen feet. A small opening can be seen near the top of the chimney, on the
west side. The small rocks jutting out from the walls look as if they would
serve as pretty good hand holds.
name: Natural Chimney

desc:
This ledge is located at the very top of a natural rock chimney. The ledge
follows the steep, rocky hillside to the west. The chimney-like tunnel
disappears into the dark below. It looks like a good place to sit, but the view
is largely blocked by the hills. The ledge is covered with bird droppings, and
even more so towards the west along the ledge.
name: Small Shelf

desc:
This long but low tunnel stretches through dark gravel and grey rock in a
north-south direction. The tunnel is gradually getting darker and narrower as it
crawls deeper under the hills. The ground looks surprisingly smooth for what
looks like a natural cavern.
name: Cavern Entrance

desc:
White, sticky spider webs have been woven throughout this round cavern. The
thread is the most dense at the south side of the cavern, from where the
individual strings reach out along the walls and through the open space, to
block and trap anything entering the lair. Where the cavern's walls are visible
behind the web, they are of solid grey rock. The only exit is a tunnel leading
to the north.
name: Web-covered Cavern

desc:
The ceiling in this cavern looms but ten feet above, but what the cavern lacks
in height, it makes up for in width as it extends atleast thirty feet in all
directions. There are several outcroppings hanging down from the ceiling, which
contributes to casting the cavern in deep shadows. The floor is covered in a
thick layer of fine sand, and centuries of accumulated bat droppings. A crevice
slopes up to the west toward the only visible exit from the cave.
name: Shady Cavern

desc:
A wild and overgrown hedge surrounds this ruined farmstead. Moss-covered
foundations of buildings remain amidst primroses and willows. The inhabitants
must have abandoned this place a long time ago, since all the wooden structures
have rotted away. A fading gravel path leads to the south through a hedge.
name: Among Moss-covered Ruins

desc:
A gloomy room, with mould and mildew colonizing the walls lies beneath the
remains of a farmhouse. The floor has a thick carpeting of dust with pieces of
rotten wood and broken pieces of pottery littering the ground. There are cobwebs
on the ceiling shifting slightly in the disturbed air.
name: Cellar

desc:
These fields may once have yielded great harvests of wheat, potatoes, or
whatever else those working the fields planted. Those days are gone and the
fields are now abandoned, and nothing grow here but weed and wildflowers. The
fields continue to the north and west, while a ruined village lies to the south.
There is a ruin of a small house just adjacent to the fields to the east.
name: Abandoned Fields

desc:
The floor in this ruin is made of compacted earth. Only the walls remain of the
ruin to the north and south, and the ceiling collapsed long ago. Wreckage from
the collapsed roof, consisting mostly of broken pieces of wooden planks, litter
the ground. The walls to the east and west remain largely intact, though they
are scorched from an old fire. The walls are painted red, and wherever the fire
did not blacken them, the paint is flaking badly.
name: Ruined Hut

desc:
The grass on this field is tall and green. It sways gently whenever a breeze
picks up. The land is mostly flat but a few low hills rise in gentle waves to
the west. The field looks as if it has never been cultivated. The grass grows
wild and several large, sprawling bushes dot the landscape.
name: Fields of Swaying Grass

desc:
These fields were once farmed, likely by the people that inhabited the ruined
village to the east. Now even the fences around the fields are mostly gone, and
in the fields themselves, nothing grow but weed and wildflowers. Although most
of the village lies to the east, there is a ruin of one old farmhouse much
closer, just to the north.
name: Abandoned Fields

desc:
The walls, floor, and ceiling of this musty cavern all consist of dark brown
earth. The cave can not be far underground, because roots poke through the
ceiling. Upon the ground are several small, broken eggs that sit amongst several
ghost-white snake skins.
name: Earthy Cavern

desc:
There is a small clearing here in this small clump of bushes. The ground is
carpeted by flowers, and bees flit from one to the next, gathering the golden
treasure within. There is a large beehive in the middle of the clearing, from
which comes a very distinct buzzing.
name: Small Clearing in the Bushes

desc:
A narrow, weather-beaten rope bridge has been constructed over a shallow but
steep chasm. It provides a path between the flat, rocky area to the east and the
taller ridgeline to the west. Around the bridge, the hilltops of Emyn Beraid
extend in all directions. The bridge is rickety and poorly constructed and would
probably not hold much weight, it looks like something a group of people would
want to cross one at a time.
name: Narrow, Wooden Rope-bridge

desc:
Steep hillsides rise abruptly around this narrow ravine, forming a dead-end
where the only way out is to the north. The ground is covered with small rocks
and gravel, and there is no vegetation in this bare place. Above, a rickety
looking rope-bridge has been constructed as a pathway across the ravine.
name: In a Ravine

desc:
A long slope from a hilltop to the south comes to an end here atop these bluffs.
The bluffs overlook the surrounding area and hills cover the land to the north,
west, and east. The side of the cliff to the north is very steep and does not
look like it would provide reliable footing. On top of the bluff stands an old,
grey cottage made of stone.
name: Atop Bluffs

desc:
Dark, still water fills this small cave. The water seems stale as if it does not
circulate much. The bottom of the cave is covered by a thin layer of mud, and
there are several pieces of some kind of cloth or leather half-buried in the
mud, rotting away.
name: Underwater Grave

desc:
Meandering in from the west, the goat trail descends towards the south, and the
hoof marks in the soil almost vanish in the more grass-covered areas. A large
shrubbery of holly bushes completely covers one small hilltop. A lonely birch
rises from the centre of the thicket, at the apex of the hill.
name: Meandering Goat Trail

desc:
Here, in the great heights of the Tower Hills, on a steep hillside, is a nest of
some bird of prey. The nest has been built between a boulder and the rocky wall,
providing a safe spot against the wind and sun. It's constructed of numerous
branches, grass and other vegetation. It is not possible to continue anywhere
but down from here, where a small meandering path snakes left and right
following the shape of the rocky hillside.
name: Steep Hillside in Great Heights

desc:
Low but sheer cliffs rise to the north and west, bordering this section of
plains. Grass and wildflowers grow rampant here, but springing up in many places
are a wide variety of thorny bushes. There are several species of blackberries,
along with knotberries, dewberries, wild rose bushes, and many others. Barbed
vines wind their way beneath the grass, threatening to trip unwary travellers.
name: Thorns and Briars

desc:
Large rocks and boulders have broken free from the hillsides to the north and
west, coming to rest at the edge of a broad plain. Many species of wild bushes
have made their home here, squeezing in between the rocks and clinging to every
patch of soil they can find. While the bushes grow clustered in tangled messes,
there is ample room to move between the thickets with ease.
name: Tangled Bushes

desc:
The air in this cavern is thick and musty, and strangly warm. The ground is
covered in a thick blanket of green and brown moss; so thick and supple is the
mossy carpet that even the sound of footsteps become muted, lending to the
crypt-like silence that pervades the cavern. Small roots protrude from the
earthen walls, intertwining together to make ominous, twisted shapes.
name: Musty Cavern

desc:
A low, yet dense forest grows towards the south, where the tops of various trees
intermingle to form a palette of dark greens. The ground here is hilly, with a
few large rocks protruding from the ground. Towards the west, a thick, natural
wall of jagged rocks thrusts up from the ground.
name: Fields before a Forest

desc:
A few trees grow on this plain, widely separated by green and brown grass. There
are few signs of animal activity here, and there are no man-made structures in
sight in any direction. There is, however, an old trail to the north, the only
thing that stands out on these featureless plains.
name: Sparsely Forested Plain
